rubric.specific_prediction: LLM agents using structured memory representations (e.g., knowledge graphs, belief graphs, attribute-indexed retrieval) outperform agents using unstructured memory (e.g., naive concatenation of full history or raw episodic logs) on complex text games with non-trivial state spaces and partial observability.
operational_signals: Direct comparison between structured memory architectures (graphs, schemas, attributes) and unstructured/concatenation baselines
Performance metrics such as task completion rate, score, success rate, or goal achievement in text games
Ablation studies removing or replacing structured memory with full-history or unstructured approaches
rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must directly compare a structured memory method against an unstructured or naive concatenation baseline on the same text game environment, reporting quantitative task performance metrics.
rubric.what_does_support_look_like: Structured memory agents achieve significantly higher task completion rates, scores, or success rates than unstructured/naive concatenation baselines in complex or long-horizon text games.
rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Unstructured or naive concatenation methods match or exceed the performance of structured memory methods on complex text games with non-trivial state spaces.

rubric.specific_prediction: LLM agents with selective, contextual memory retrieval mechanisms (e.g., semantic similarity, attribute filtering, graph traversal) outperform agents that use brute-force context inclusion or no retrieval filtering in text games requiring planning and reasoning.
operational_signals: Comparison between selective/contextual retrieval methods and full-history or no-retrieval baselines
Explicit ablation of retrieval mechanisms (e.g., removing semantic retrieval, attribute filtering)
Performance metrics in text games, particularly those with long horizons or large state spaces
rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must compare an agent with selective retrieval (e.g., embedding-based, attribute-based, or graph-based selection) against a baseline using full context or no retrieval on the same task, with quantitative performance metrics.
rubric.what_does_support_look_like: Selective retrieval agents demonstrate higher task performance and better scaling to complex or long-horizon games compared to full-history or no-retrieval baselines.
rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Full-history inclusion or no-retrieval baselines achieve equal or superior performance to selective retrieval methods in complex text games.

rubric.specific_prediction: LLM agents that combine structured memory (e.g., graphs, schemas) with episodic/reflection-based memory outperform agents relying solely on one memory modality (structured-only or episodic-only) in complex text games.
operational_signals: Ablation studies comparing hybrid memory systems against structured-only or episodic-only variants
Explicit architectural comparison of combined vs. single-modality memory systems
Performance metrics in text games with partial observability and long planning horizons
rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must compare a hybrid memory architecture (combining structured and episodic/reflection components) against single-modality baselines (structured-only or episodic-only) on the same text game task with quantitative metrics.
rubric.what_does_support_look_like: Hybrid memory systems achieve higher task completion rates or scores than either structured-only or episodic-only memory systems in complex text games.
rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Single-modality memory systems (structured-only or episodic-only) match or exceed the performance of hybrid memory architectures in complex text games.

rubric.specific_prediction: As text game complexity increases (larger state/action spaces, longer horizons, more partial observability), the performance gap between structured/selective memory agents and unstructured/naive memory agents widens in favor of structured/selective approaches.
operational_signals: Performance comparisons across multiple difficulty levels or game complexities
Evaluation on simple vs. complex game variants with varying state space sizes or horizon lengths
Explicit analysis of scaling behavior as task complexity increases
rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must evaluate both structured/selective and unstructured/naive memory approaches across at least two distinct complexity levels of text games, showing performance trends as complexity increases.
rubric.what_does_support_look_like: The performance advantage of structured/selective memory over unstructured/naive methods increases as game complexity (state space, horizon, partial observability) increases.
rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Unstructured/naive memory methods maintain equal performance gaps or close the gap with structured/selective methods as game complexity increases.

rubric.specific_prediction: In text games where full history fits within the LLM context window and state spaces are small, advanced structured memory and selective retrieval provide minimal or no performance improvement over simple full-history inclusion.
operational_signals: Comparison of advanced memory architectures vs. full-history baselines on small-scale or simple text games
Explicit evaluation on tasks where context limits are not reached
Performance metrics showing marginal differences in low-complexity settings
rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must compare advanced memory/retrieval methods against full-history baselines on trivial or small-scale text games where history fits in context, reporting performance metrics that show minimal or no advantage.
rubric.what_does_support_look_like: In small-scale games where full history fits in context, advanced memory structures show minimal (<5% relative) or no performance improvement over full-history baselines.
rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Advanced memory structures show substantial (>10% relative) performance gains over full-history baselines even in trivial settings where all history fits in context.

theory_statement_law.theory_name: Hybrid Structured-Episodic Memory as a Fundamental Requirement for Robust Performance in LLM-Based Text Game Agents
theory_statement_law.theory_description: This theory posits that robust performance, generalization, and strategic flexibility in language model (LLM) agents across complex, partially observable text games require not just large memory but a principled integration of structured (e.g., knowledge/belief graph) and episodic/reflection-based memory. The synergy emerges because structured memory offers compact relational representations for navigation and reasoning, while episodic/reflection memory provides dynamic adaptation, procedural recall, and learning from both failure and success. Neither naive retention of all history nor pure reliance on unstructured episodic memory alone is sufficient for scaling or generalizing to new, difficult, or long-horizon tasks; selective retrieval and composition of these modalities is necessary to balance information compression, retrieval relevance, and depth of reasoning.

law.statement_name: Memory Structure, Selectivity, and Retrieval are as Critical as Memory Size or Modality
law.theory_statement: The benefit of memory augmentation to LLM agents in text games depends not just on the presence or volume of stored experience, but crucially on how memory is structured (graphs, attributes, summaries), accessed (contextual semantic retrieval, attribute filtering, embedding similarity, graph traversal), and composed/interleaved with ongoing reasoning. Unstructured, brute-force context window or naive concatenation yields information overload or sparsity and fails to scale in complex games.
law.domain_scope: LLM-based agents with any form of persistent memory in settings with nontrivial state/action space, partial observability, and long or branching planning horizons.
special_cases: In trivial or small-scale settings where history fits in the prompt, advanced retrieval offers little marginal gain.
In environments with no discoverable structure or pure randomness, selectivity/structure add overhead but little task benefit.

supporting_evidence.text: Worldformer’s graph-diff representation and set-of-sequences (SOS) loss, RAP’s windowed trajectory retrieval, MemInsight’s attribute-based and embedding retrieval, and MC-DML’s distinction between context window and cross-trial memory all yield large performance improvements over naive retrieval or memory-dumping agents.
Ariadne/AriGraph's ablations (full-history, summaries, RAG, Simulacra) and unstructured context concatenation produce much lower performance and poor scaling to complex or long tasks.
ThinkThrice and Sweet&Sour explicitly leverage retrieval, selective reflection, and verification to outperform memoryless or unselective/naive retrieval agents.

GATA, KG-A2C, and MoE agents' use of graph-based masking, attention, and value-seeded mixing demonstrates the criticality of retrieval mechanisms and selective structuring, as opposed to raw memory augmentation.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Importance of retrieval is acknowledged in information retrieval, RAG, and some memory-augmented LM architectures; information overload in full context is a recognized issue.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: This general law formalizes the equivalence (or superiority) of memory structure and access relative to raw memory volume or memory type for performance/goals in complex reasoning environments, based on unified evidence.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Evidence exists in various ablations and isolated system papers, but this theory codifies it at a high level and ties it to both failure and success modes systematically.
references: Borji et al. (2023) A Survey on Large Language Model-Based Game Agents [retrieval cited, not law]
Meng et al. (2025) MemInsight [attribute/embedding retrieval]
Yao et al. (2023) RAG survey and retrieval theory

law.theory_name: Hybrid Structured-Episodic Memory as a Fundamental Requirement for Robust Performance in LLM-Based Text Game Agents
law.theory_description: This theory posits that robust performance, generalization, and strategic flexibility in language model (LLM) agents across complex, partially observable text games require not just large memory but a principled integration of structured (e.g., knowledge/belief graph) and episodic/reflection-based memory. The synergy emerges because structured memory offers compact relational representations for navigation and reasoning, while episodic/reflection memory provides dynamic adaptation, procedural recall, and learning from both failure and success. Neither naive retention of all history nor pure reliance on unstructured episodic memory alone is sufficient for scaling or generalizing to new, difficult, or long-horizon tasks; selective retrieval and composition of these modalities is necessary to balance information compression, retrieval relevance, and depth of reasoning.

law.statement_name: Memory Structure, Selectivity, and Retrieval are as Critical as Memory Size or Modality
law.theory_statement: The benefit of memory augmentation to LLM agents in text games depends not just on the presence or volume of stored experience, but crucially on how memory is structured (graphs, attributes, summaries), accessed (contextual semantic retrieval, attribute filtering, embedding similarity, graph traversal), and composed/interleaved with ongoing reasoning. Unstructured, brute-force context window or naive concatenation yields information overload or sparsity and fails to scale in complex games.
law.domain_scope: LLM-based agents with any form of persistent memory in settings with nontrivial state/action space, partial observability, and long or branching planning horizons.
special_cases: In trivial or small-scale settings where history fits in the prompt, advanced retrieval offers little marginal gain.
In environments with no discoverable structure or pure randomness, selectivity/structure add overhead but little task benefit.

supporting_evidence.text: Worldformer’s graph-diff representation and set-of-sequences (SOS) loss, RAP’s windowed trajectory retrieval, MemInsight’s attribute-based and embedding retrieval, and MC-DML’s distinction between context window and cross-trial memory all yield large performance improvements over naive retrieval or memory-dumping agents.
Ariadne/AriGraph's ablations (full-history, summaries, RAG, Simulacra) and unstructured context concatenation produce much lower performance and poor scaling to complex or long tasks.
ThinkThrice and Sweet&Sour explicitly leverage retrieval, selective reflection, and verification to outperform memoryless or unselective/naive retrieval agents.

GATA, KG-A2C, and MoE agents' use of graph-based masking, attention, and value-seeded mixing demonstrates the criticality of retrieval mechanisms and selective structuring, as opposed to raw memory augmentation.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Importance of retrieval is acknowledged in information retrieval, RAG, and some memory-augmented LM architectures; information overload in full context is a recognized issue.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: This general law formalizes the equivalence (or superiority) of memory structure and access relative to raw memory volume or memory type for performance/goals in complex reasoning environments, based on unified evidence.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Evidence exists in various ablations and isolated system papers, but this theory codifies it at a high level and ties it to both failure and success modes systematically.
references: Borji et al. (2023) A Survey on Large Language Model-Based Game Agents [retrieval cited, not law]
Meng et al. (2025) MemInsight [attribute/embedding retrieval]
Yao et al. (2023) RAG survey and retrieval theory

predictive_rubric.specific_prediction: LLM agents using structured memory representations (e.g., knowledge graphs, belief graphs, attribute-indexed retrieval) outperform agents using unstructured memory (e.g., naive concatenation of full history or raw episodic logs) on complex text games with non-trivial state spaces and partial observability.
operational_signals: Direct comparison between structured memory architectures (graphs, schemas, attributes) and unstructured/concatenation baselines
Performance metrics such as task completion rate, score, success rate, or goal achievement in text games
Ablation studies removing or replacing structured memory with full-history or unstructured approaches
predictive_rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must directly compare a structured memory method against an unstructured or naive concatenation baseline on the same text game environment, reporting quantitative task performance metrics.
predictive_rubric.what_does_support_look_like: Structured memory agents achieve significantly higher task completion rates, scores, or success rates than unstructured/naive concatenation baselines in complex or long-horizon text games.
predictive_rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Unstructured or naive concatenation methods match or exceed the performance of structured memory methods on complex text games with non-trivial state spaces.

predictive_rubric.specific_prediction: LLM agents with selective, contextual memory retrieval mechanisms (e.g., semantic similarity, attribute filtering, graph traversal) outperform agents that use brute-force context inclusion or no retrieval filtering in text games requiring planning and reasoning.

Explicit ablation of retrieval mechanisms (e.g., removing semantic retrieval, attribute filtering)
Performance metrics in text games, particularly those with long horizons or large state spaces
predictive_rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must compare an agent with selective retrieval (e.g., embedding-based, attribute-based, or graph-based selection) against a baseline using full context or no retrieval on the same task, with quantitative performance metrics.
predictive_rubric.what_does_support_look_like: Selective retrieval agents demonstrate higher task performance and better scaling to complex or long-horizon games compared to full-history or no-retrieval baselines.
predictive_rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Full-history inclusion or no-retrieval baselines achieve equal or superior performance to selective retrieval methods in complex text games.

law.theory_name: Hybrid Structured-Episodic Memory as a Fundamental Requirement for Robust Performance in LLM-Based Text Game Agents
law.theory_description: This theory posits that robust performance, generalization, and strategic flexibility in language model (LLM) agents across complex, partially observable text games require not just large memory but a principled integration of structured (e.g., knowledge/belief graph) and episodic/reflection-based memory. The synergy emerges because structured memory offers compact relational representations for navigation and reasoning, while episodic/reflection memory provides dynamic adaptation, procedural recall, and learning from both failure and success. Neither naive retention of all history nor pure reliance on unstructured episodic memory alone is sufficient for scaling or generalizing to new, difficult, or long-horizon tasks; selective retrieval and composition of these modalities is necessary to balance information compression, retrieval relevance, and depth of reasoning.

law.statement_name: Memory Structure, Selectivity, and Retrieval are as Critical as Memory Size or Modality
law.theory_statement: The benefit of memory augmentation to LLM agents in text games depends not just on the presence or volume of stored experience, but crucially on how memory is structured (graphs, attributes, summaries), accessed (contextual semantic retrieval, attribute filtering, embedding similarity, graph traversal), and composed/interleaved with ongoing reasoning. Unstructured, brute-force context window or naive concatenation yields information overload or sparsity and fails to scale in complex games.
law.domain_scope: LLM-based agents with any form of persistent memory in settings with nontrivial state/action space, partial observability, and long or branching planning horizons.
special_cases: In trivial or small-scale settings where history fits in the prompt, advanced retrieval offers little marginal gain.
In environments with no discoverable structure or pure randomness, selectivity/structure add overhead but little task benefit.

supporting_evidence.text: Worldformer’s graph-diff representation and set-of-sequences (SOS) loss, RAP’s windowed trajectory retrieval, MemInsight’s attribute-based and embedding retrieval, and MC-DML’s distinction between context window and cross-trial memory all yield large performance improvements over naive retrieval or memory-dumping agents.
Ariadne/AriGraph's ablations (full-history, summaries, RAG, Simulacra) and unstructured context concatenation produce much lower performance and poor scaling to complex or long tasks.
ThinkThrice and Sweet&Sour explicitly leverage retrieval, selective reflection, and verification to outperform memoryless or unselective/naive retrieval agents.

GATA, KG-A2C, and MoE agents' use of graph-based masking, attention, and value-seeded mixing demonstrates the criticality of retrieval mechanisms and selective structuring, as opposed to raw memory augmentation.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Importance of retrieval is acknowledged in information retrieval, RAG, and some memory-augmented LM architectures; information overload in full context is a recognized issue.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: This general law formalizes the equivalence (or superiority) of memory structure and access relative to raw memory volume or memory type for performance/goals in complex reasoning environments, based on unified evidence.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Evidence exists in various ablations and isolated system papers, but this theory codifies it at a high level and ties it to both failure and success modes systematically.
references: Borji et al. (2023) A Survey on Large Language Model-Based Game Agents [retrieval cited, not law]
Meng et al. (2025) MemInsight [attribute/embedding retrieval]
Yao et al. (2023) RAG survey and retrieval theory

predictive_rubric.specific_prediction: LLM agents using structured memory representations (e.g., knowledge graphs, belief graphs, attribute-indexed retrieval) outperform agents using unstructured memory (e.g., naive concatenation of full history or raw episodic logs) on complex text games with non-trivial state spaces and partial observability.
operational_signals: Direct comparison between structured memory architectures (graphs, schemas, attributes) and unstructured/concatenation baselines
Performance metrics such as task completion rate, score, success rate, or goal achievement in text games
Ablation studies removing or replacing structured memory with full-history or unstructured approaches
predictive_rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must directly compare a structured memory method against an unstructured or naive concatenation baseline on the same text game environment, reporting quantitative task performance metrics.
predictive_rubric.what_does_support_look_like: Structured memory agents achieve significantly higher task completion rates, scores, or success rates than unstructured/naive concatenation baselines in complex or long-horizon text games.
predictive_rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Unstructured or naive concatenation methods match or exceed the performance of structured memory methods on complex text games with non-trivial state spaces.

law.theory_name: Hybrid Structured-Episodic Memory as a Fundamental Requirement for Robust Performance in LLM-Based Text Game Agents
law.theory_description: This theory posits that robust performance, generalization, and strategic flexibility in language model (LLM) agents across complex, partially observable text games require not just large memory but a principled integration of structured (e.g., knowledge/belief graph) and episodic/reflection-based memory. The synergy emerges because structured memory offers compact relational representations for navigation and reasoning, while episodic/reflection memory provides dynamic adaptation, procedural recall, and learning from both failure and success. Neither naive retention of all history nor pure reliance on unstructured episodic memory alone is sufficient for scaling or generalizing to new, difficult, or long-horizon tasks; selective retrieval and composition of these modalities is necessary to balance information compression, retrieval relevance, and depth of reasoning.

law.statement_name: Memory Structure, Selectivity, and Retrieval are as Critical as Memory Size or Modality
law.theory_statement: The benefit of memory augmentation to LLM agents in text games depends not just on the presence or volume of stored experience, but crucially on how memory is structured (graphs, attributes, summaries), accessed (contextual semantic retrieval, attribute filtering, embedding similarity, graph traversal), and composed/interleaved with ongoing reasoning. Unstructured, brute-force context window or naive concatenation yields information overload or sparsity and fails to scale in complex games.
law.domain_scope: LLM-based agents with any form of persistent memory in settings with nontrivial state/action space, partial observability, and long or branching planning horizons.
special_cases: In trivial or small-scale settings where history fits in the prompt, advanced retrieval offers little marginal gain.
In environments with no discoverable structure or pure randomness, selectivity/structure add overhead but little task benefit.

supporting_evidence.text: Worldformer’s graph-diff representation and set-of-sequences (SOS) loss, RAP’s windowed trajectory retrieval, MemInsight’s attribute-based and embedding retrieval, and MC-DML’s distinction between context window and cross-trial memory all yield large performance improvements over naive retrieval or memory-dumping agents.
Ariadne/AriGraph's ablations (full-history, summaries, RAG, Simulacra) and unstructured context concatenation produce much lower performance and poor scaling to complex or long tasks.
ThinkThrice and Sweet&Sour explicitly leverage retrieval, selective reflection, and verification to outperform memoryless or unselective/naive retrieval agents.

GATA, KG-A2C, and MoE agents' use of graph-based masking, attention, and value-seeded mixing demonstrates the criticality of retrieval mechanisms and selective structuring, as opposed to raw memory augmentation.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Importance of retrieval is acknowledged in information retrieval, RAG, and some memory-augmented LM architectures; information overload in full context is a recognized issue.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: This general law formalizes the equivalence (or superiority) of memory structure and access relative to raw memory volume or memory type for performance/goals in complex reasoning environments, based on unified evidence.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Evidence exists in various ablations and isolated system papers, but this theory codifies it at a high level and ties it to both failure and success modes systematically.
references: Borji et al. (2023) A Survey on Large Language Model-Based Game Agents [retrieval cited, not law]
Meng et al. (2025) MemInsight [attribute/embedding retrieval]
Yao et al. (2023) RAG survey and retrieval theory

predictive_evaluation.evidence_quote_or_locator: Table 1, comparing LTMemory (semantic retrieval) vs FUMemory (full history concatenation) and GAMemory (weighted retrieval) vs baselines across HotpotQA datasets. Section 5.2 reports: 'Our model with on-policy optimization outperforms other baselines in most cases' with Ours-on achieving 0.3186 vs FUMemory 0.3451 on Hard/GPT-4o-mini, but 0.4037 vs 0.2844 on Medium/Qwen-2.5.
predictive_evaluation.evidence_in_support: Table 1 shows that selective retrieval methods (LTMemory, GAMemory, and the proposed methods with MoE gate function) generally outperform FUMemory (brute-force full history concatenation) on medium and easy difficulty levels, particularly with Qwen-2.5 and Llama-3.1 models. For example, on HotpotQA-Medium with Qwen-2.5, Ours-on achieves 0.4037 vs FUMemory's 0.2844.

predictive_evaluation.evidence_quote_or_locator: Table 1, comparing performance across HotpotQA-Hard, HotpotQA-Medium, and HotpotQA-Easy difficulty levels for all memory methods. Section 5.2 notes: 'it appears that some LLMs exhibit limited dependence on memory for easy-level questions, possibly because they have encountered the necessary references to these questions in their pre-training corpus.'
predictive_evaluation.evidence_in_contradiction: Table 1 shows that as task complexity decreases (Hard → Medium → Easy), the performance advantage of advanced memory methods (including selective retrieval methods like Ours-on, GAMemory, LTMemory) does not consistently widen compared to simpler methods. In fact, on Easy tasks with GPT-4o-mini, FUMemory (0.3645) performs comparably to or sometimes better than more sophisticated methods. The paper notes that on easy questions, 'some LLMs exhibit limited dependence on memory,' contradicting the prediction that the gap should widen with complexity.

predictive_evaluation.evidence_quote_or_locator: Table 1 HotpotQA-Easy results and Section 5.2 discussion of easy-level questions

law.theory_name: Hybrid Structured-Episodic Memory as a Fundamental Requirement for Robust Performance in LLM-Based Text Game Agents
law.theory_description: This theory posits that robust performance, generalization, and strategic flexibility in language model (LLM) agents across complex, partially observable text games require not just large memory but a principled integration of structured (e.g., knowledge/belief graph) and episodic/reflection-based memory. The synergy emerges because structured memory offers compact relational representations for navigation and reasoning, while episodic/reflection memory provides dynamic adaptation, procedural recall, and learning from both failure and success. Neither naive retention of all history nor pure reliance on unstructured episodic memory alone is sufficient for scaling or generalizing to new, difficult, or long-horizon tasks; selective retrieval and composition of these modalities is necessary to balance information compression, retrieval relevance, and depth of reasoning.

law.statement_name: Memory Structure, Selectivity, and Retrieval are as Critical as Memory Size or Modality
law.theory_statement: The benefit of memory augmentation to LLM agents in text games depends not just on the presence or volume of stored experience, but crucially on how memory is structured (graphs, attributes, summaries), accessed (contextual semantic retrieval, attribute filtering, embedding similarity, graph traversal), and composed/interleaved with ongoing reasoning. Unstructured, brute-force context window or naive concatenation yields information overload or sparsity and fails to scale in complex games.
law.domain_scope: LLM-based agents with any form of persistent memory in settings with nontrivial state/action space, partial observability, and long or branching planning horizons.
special_cases: In trivial or small-scale settings where history fits in the prompt, advanced retrieval offers little marginal gain.
In environments with no discoverable structure or pure randomness, selectivity/structure add overhead but little task benefit.

supporting_evidence.text: Worldformer’s graph-diff representation and set-of-sequences (SOS) loss, RAP’s windowed trajectory retrieval, MemInsight’s attribute-based and embedding retrieval, and MC-DML’s distinction between context window and cross-trial memory all yield large performance improvements over naive retrieval or memory-dumping agents.
Ariadne/AriGraph's ablations (full-history, summaries, RAG, Simulacra) and unstructured context concatenation produce much lower performance and poor scaling to complex or long tasks.
ThinkThrice and Sweet&Sour explicitly leverage retrieval, selective reflection, and verification to outperform memoryless or unselective/naive retrieval agents.

GATA, KG-A2C, and MoE agents' use of graph-based masking, attention, and value-seeded mixing demonstrates the criticality of retrieval mechanisms and selective structuring, as opposed to raw memory augmentation.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Importance of retrieval is acknowledged in information retrieval, RAG, and some memory-augmented LM architectures; information overload in full context is a recognized issue.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: This general law formalizes the equivalence (or superiority) of memory structure and access relative to raw memory volume or memory type for performance/goals in complex reasoning environments, based on unified evidence.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Evidence exists in various ablations and isolated system papers, but this theory codifies it at a high level and ties it to both failure and success modes systematically.
references: Borji et al. (2023) A Survey on Large Language Model-Based Game Agents [retrieval cited, not law]
Meng et al. (2025) MemInsight [attribute/embedding retrieval]
Yao et al. (2023) RAG survey and retrieval theory

predictive_rubric.specific_prediction: LLM agents using structured memory representations (e.g., knowledge graphs, belief graphs, attribute-indexed retrieval) outperform agents using unstructured memory (e.g., naive concatenation of full history or raw episodic logs) on complex text games with non-trivial state spaces and partial observability.
operational_signals: Direct comparison between structured memory architectures (graphs, schemas, attributes) and unstructured/concatenation baselines
Performance metrics such as task completion rate, score, success rate, or goal achievement in text games
Ablation studies removing or replacing structured memory with full-history or unstructured approaches
predictive_rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must directly compare a structured memory method against an unstructured or naive concatenation baseline on the same text game environment, reporting quantitative task performance metrics.
predictive_rubric.what_does_support_look_like: Structured memory agents achieve significantly higher task completion rates, scores, or success rates than unstructured/naive concatenation baselines in complex or long-horizon text games.
predictive_rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Unstructured or naive concatenation methods match or exceed the performance of structured memory methods on complex text games with non-trivial state spaces.

predictive_rubric.specific_prediction: LLM agents with selective, contextual memory retrieval mechanisms (e.g., semantic similarity, attribute filtering, graph traversal) outperform agents that use brute-force context inclusion or no retrieval filtering in text games requiring planning and reasoning.
operational_signals: Comparison between selective/contextual retrieval methods and full-history or no-retrieval baselines
Explicit ablation of retrieval mechanisms (e.g., removing semantic retrieval, attribute filtering)
Performance metrics in text games, particularly those with long horizons or large state spaces
predictive_rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must compare an agent with selective retrieval (e.g., embedding-based, attribute-based, or graph-based selection) against a baseline using full context or no retrieval on the same task, with quantitative performance metrics.
predictive_rubric.what_does_support_look_like: Selective retrieval agents demonstrate higher task performance and better scaling to complex or long-horizon games compared to full-history or no-retrieval baselines.
predictive_rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Full-history inclusion or no-retrieval baselines achieve equal or superior performance to selective retrieval methods in complex text games.

predictive_rubric.specific_prediction: LLM agents that combine structured memory (e.g., graphs, schemas) with episodic/reflection-based memory outperform agents relying solely on one memory modality (structured-only or episodic-only) in complex text games.
operational_signals: Ablation studies comparing hybrid memory systems against structured-only or episodic-only variants
Explicit architectural comparison of combined vs. single-modality memory systems
Performance metrics in text games with partial observability and long planning horizons
predictive_rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must compare a hybrid memory architecture (combining structured and episodic/reflection components) against single-modality baselines (structured-only or episodic-only) on the same text game task with quantitative metrics.
predictive_rubric.what_does_support_look_like: Hybrid memory systems achieve higher task completion rates or scores than either structured-only or episodic-only memory systems in complex text games.
predictive_rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Single-modality memory systems (structured-only or episodic-only) match or exceed the performance of hybrid memory architectures in complex text games.

predictive_rubric.specific_prediction: As text game complexity increases (larger state/action spaces, longer horizons, more partial observability), the performance gap between structured/selective memory agents and unstructured/naive memory agents widens in favor of structured/selective approaches.
operational_signals: Performance comparisons across multiple difficulty levels or game complexities
Evaluation on simple vs. complex game variants with varying state space sizes or horizon lengths
Explicit analysis of scaling behavior as task complexity increases
predictive_rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must evaluate both structured/selective and unstructured/naive memory approaches across at least two distinct complexity levels of text games, showing performance trends as complexity increases.
predictive_rubric.what_does_support_look_like: The performance advantage of structured/selective memory over unstructured/naive methods increases as game complexity (state space, horizon, partial observability) increases.
predictive_rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Unstructured/naive memory methods maintain equal performance gaps or close the gap with structured/selective methods as game complexity increases.

predictive_rubric.specific_prediction: In text games where full history fits within the LLM context window and state spaces are small, advanced structured memory and selective retrieval provide minimal or no performance improvement over simple full-history inclusion.
operational_signals: Comparison of advanced memory architectures vs. full-history baselines on small-scale or simple text games
Explicit evaluation on tasks where context limits are not reached
Performance metrics showing marginal differences in low-complexity settings
predictive_rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must compare advanced memory/retrieval methods against full-history baselines on trivial or small-scale text games where history fits in context, reporting performance metrics that show minimal or no advantage.
predictive_rubric.what_does_support_look_like: In small-scale games where full history fits in context, advanced memory structures show minimal (<5% relative) or no performance improvement over full-history baselines.
predictive_rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Advanced memory structures show substantial (>10% relative) performance gains over full-history baselines even in trivial settings where all history fits in context.

predictive_evaluation.evidence_quote_or_locator: Table 1 compares SUMER (with selective semantic and keyword search tools) against 'Full Context' baseline and RAG baseline. SUMER-GRPO achieves 66.79 J vs Full Context 46.69 J and RAG 35.84 J overall. Section 4.2 states 'SUMER trained with GRPO...nearly doubling judge accuracy relative to the strongest compression-based baseline' and improves J from 48.55 (pre-RL baseline SUMER-Base with search) to 66.79.
predictive_evaluation.evidence_in_support: SUMER with selective retrieval mechanisms (semantic search via embeddings and keyword search) substantially outperforms the Full Context baseline (which uses brute-force context inclusion) across all question types: overall J of 66.79 vs 46.69, representing a 43% improvement. The paper explicitly compares selective retrieval against full-history inclusion with quantitative performance metrics on the same task.

predictive_evaluation.evidence_quote_or_locator: Section 5.1 states: 'Our experimental setup also has practical limitations...the LoCoMo dataset we study does not exceed the base model's context window, so our experiments do not fully capture the regime where the conversation history is vastly longer than what can be naively fed into the model.'

predictive_rubric.specific_prediction: In text games where full history fits within the LLM context window and state spaces are small, advanced structured memory and selective retrieval provide minimal or no performance improvement over simple full-history inclusion.
operational_signals: Comparison of advanced memory architectures vs. full-history baselines on small-scale or simple text games
Explicit evaluation on tasks where context limits are not reached
Performance metrics showing marginal differences in low-complexity settings
predictive_rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must compare advanced memory/retrieval methods against full-history baselines on trivial or small-scale text games where history fits in context, reporting performance metrics that show minimal or no advantage.
predictive_rubric.what_does_support_look_like: In small-scale games where full history fits in context, advanced memory structures show minimal (<5% relative) or no performance improvement over full-history baselines.

predictive_evaluation.evidence_quote_or_locator: Table 2, Section 4 (Performance Comparison), and Section 5 (Discussion on Direct RAG comparison). Table 2 compares O-Mem (structured memory with persona attributes, episodic memory, and working memory) against baselines including Mem0, MemoryOS, and A-Mem. Table 5 compares O-Mem against 'Direct RAG' which retrieves from raw, unstructured interaction history.
predictive_evaluation.evidence_in_support: O-Mem (structured memory system) achieves 51.67% F1 on LoCoMo with GPT-4.1, significantly outperforming unstructured baselines: Mem0 (25.40% F1), MemoryOS (38.58% F1), and A-Mem (33.78% F1). Table 5 shows O-Mem (51.67% F1) also outperforms Direct RAG on complete raw interaction history (50.25% F1), demonstrating that structured memory representations outperform unstructured concatenation approaches.

predictive_evaluation.evidence_quote_or_locator: Table 5 (Direct RAG comparison) and Section 5 (Discussion). Direct RAG represents brute-force retrieval from complete interaction history, while O-Mem employs selective retrieval mechanisms (semantic similarity for persona memory, keyword-based filtering for episodic memory, topic-based for working memory).
predictive_evaluation.evidence_in_support: O-Mem with selective retrieval (51.67% F1) outperforms Direct RAG with full-history retrieval (50.25% F1) on LoCoMo benchmark. Furthermore, O-Mem achieves this with significantly lower token consumption (1.5K vs 2.6K tokens) and reduced latency (2.36s vs 4.01s), demonstrating that selective retrieval provides both performance and efficiency advantages over brute-force context inclusion.

predictive_evaluation.evidence_quote_or_locator: Table 6 (Ablation study on different components of O-Mem). This table systematically compares hybrid memory (WM + EM + PM) against single-modality configurations (WM only) and partial combinations (WM + EM).
predictive_evaluation.evidence_in_support: Table 6 shows that the full hybrid system (WM + EM + PM) achieves 51.67% F1, outperforming WM-only (44.03% F1) and WM + EM without persona memory (49.62% F1). Even under token-controlled conditions that eliminate the confound of increased context, hybrid memory (51.67% F1) outperforms WM-only (46.07% F1) and WM + EM (50.10% F1), demonstrating that combining structured and episodic/reflection memory modalities provides superior performance.

predictive_evaluation.evidence_quote_or_locator: Table 2 shows performance across four categories of LoCoMo (Multi-hop, Temporal, Open-domain, Single-hop) which vary in complexity. However, these are not explicitly ordered by increasing complexity levels in the paper.

law.theory_name: Hybrid Structured-Episodic Memory as a Fundamental Requirement for Robust Performance in LLM-Based Text Game Agents
law.theory_description: This theory posits that robust performance, generalization, and strategic flexibility in language model (LLM) agents across complex, partially observable text games require not just large memory but a principled integration of structured (e.g., knowledge/belief graph) and episodic/reflection-based memory. The synergy emerges because structured memory offers compact relational representations for navigation and reasoning, while episodic/reflection memory provides dynamic adaptation, procedural recall, and learning from both failure and success. Neither naive retention of all history nor pure reliance on unstructured episodic memory alone is sufficient for scaling or generalizing to new, difficult, or long-horizon tasks; selective retrieval and composition of these modalities is necessary to balance information compression, retrieval relevance, and depth of reasoning.

law.statement_name: Memory Structure, Selectivity, and Retrieval are as Critical as Memory Size or Modality
law.theory_statement: The benefit of memory augmentation to LLM agents in text games depends not just on the presence or volume of stored experience, but crucially on how memory is structured (graphs, attributes, summaries), accessed (contextual semantic retrieval, attribute filtering, embedding similarity, graph traversal), and composed/interleaved with ongoing reasoning. Unstructured, brute-force context window or naive concatenation yields information overload or sparsity and fails to scale in complex games.
law.domain_scope: LLM-based agents with any form of persistent memory in settings with nontrivial state/action space, partial observability, and long or branching planning horizons.
special_cases: In trivial or small-scale settings where history fits in the prompt, advanced retrieval offers little marginal gain.
In environments with no discoverable structure or pure randomness, selectivity/structure add overhead but little task benefit.

supporting_evidence.text: Worldformer’s graph-diff representation and set-of-sequences (SOS) loss, RAP’s windowed trajectory retrieval, MemInsight’s attribute-based and embedding retrieval, and MC-DML’s distinction between context window and cross-trial memory all yield large performance improvements over naive retrieval or memory-dumping agents.
Ariadne/AriGraph's ablations (full-history, summaries, RAG, Simulacra) and unstructured context concatenation produce much lower performance and poor scaling to complex or long tasks.
ThinkThrice and Sweet&Sour explicitly leverage retrieval, selective reflection, and verification to outperform memoryless or unselective/naive retrieval agents.

GATA, KG-A2C, and MoE agents' use of graph-based masking, attention, and value-seeded mixing demonstrates the criticality of retrieval mechanisms and selective structuring, as opposed to raw memory augmentation.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Importance of retrieval is acknowledged in information retrieval, RAG, and some memory-augmented LM architectures; information overload in full context is a recognized issue.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: This general law formalizes the equivalence (or superiority) of memory structure and access relative to raw memory volume or memory type for performance/goals in complex reasoning environments, based on unified evidence.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Evidence exists in various ablations and isolated system papers, but this theory codifies it at a high level and ties it to both failure and success modes systematically.
references: Borji et al. (2023) A Survey on Large Language Model-Based Game Agents [retrieval cited, not law]
Meng et al. (2025) MemInsight [attribute/embedding retrieval]
Yao et al. (2023) RAG survey and retrieval theory

operational_signals: Direct comparison between structured memory architectures (graphs, schemas, attributes) and unstructured/concatenation baselines
Performance metrics such as task completion rate, score, success rate, or goal achievement in text games
Ablation studies removing or replacing structured memory with full-history or unstructured approaches

predictive_rubric.what_does_support_look_like: Structured memory agents achieve significantly higher task completion rates, scores, or success rates than unstructured/naive concatenation baselines in complex or long-horizon text games.
predictive_rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Unstructured or naive concatenation methods match or exceed the performance of structured memory methods on complex text games with non-trivial state spaces.

Performance metrics in text games, particularly those with long horizons or large state spaces

predictive_rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Full-history inclusion or no-retrieval baselines achieve equal or superior performance to selective retrieval methods in complex text games.

predictive_rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Single-modality memory systems (structured-only or episodic-only) match or exceed the performance of hybrid memory architectures in complex text games.

predictive_evaluation.evidence_quote_or_locator: Table 2 and Section 5.2, where CAM (structured hierarchical memory with overlapping clustering) is compared against MemGPT and ReadAgent (unstructured tabular memory baselines) across NovelQA, QMSum, FABLES, MultiHop-RAG, ODSum-Story, and ODSum-Meeting. Table 2 shows CAM consistently outperforms unstructured baselines. Section 5.2 explicitly states: 'Structured memory approaches (e.g., RAPTOR, GraphRAG, and our CAM) demonstrate clearly stronger performance against those without explicit structures (e.g., ReadAgent and MemGPT), confirming the importance of memory structurality in reading comprehension tasks.'
predictive_evaluation.evidence_in_support: Table 2 shows CAM achieves higher performance than unstructured baselines (MemGPT, ReadAgent) across all six benchmarks. For example, on NovelQA ACC-L, CAM achieves 52.3 vs ReadAgent's 42.3 and MemGPT's 40.8. Section 5.2 explicitly confirms 'structured memory approaches...demonstrate clearly stronger performance against those without explicit structures,' directly validating that structured memory (graphs, hierarchies) outperforms unstructured/tabular approaches.

predictive_evaluation.evidence_quote_or_locator: Section 4.2 describes CAM's 'Prune-and-Grow' associative retrieval strategy and Section 5.4 (Table 3, rows 6-7) compares it against hierarchical traversal and global retrieval baselines. The paper states: 'CAM uses an LLM to select nodes from D that are helpful for answering the query...This Prune-and-Grow workflow combines global semantic matching and local structural exploration.' Table 3 shows CAM's default retrieval outperforms both hierarchical traversal and global retrieval alternatives.
predictive_evaluation.evidence_in_support: Table 3 (rows 6-7) demonstrates that CAM's selective Prune-and-Grow retrieval (row 1: NovelQA R-L 25.4, ACC-L 52.3) outperforms both hierarchical traversal (row 6: 23.2, 46.5) and global retrieval (row 7: 24.5, 50.3) baselines. Section 5.4 confirms 'using either hierarchical traversal or global retrieval leads to performance degradation, validating the effectiveness of our Prune-and-Grow strategy,' demonstrating that selective, contextual retrieval outperforms both brute-force full-context and non-selective approaches.

predictive_evaluation.evidence_quote_or_locator: Appendix F (Tables 5 and 6) analyzes performance by question complexity (single-hop, multi-hop, detail) and semantic aspects on NovelQA. The paper states CAM shows 'particularly notable gains on complex questions that require integrating semantically distant evidence.' However, this is within a single dataset, not across distinct complexity levels of different games or environments.

predictive_rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must compare advanced memory/retrieval methods against full-history baselines on trivial or small-scale text games where history fits in context, reporting performance metrics that show minimal or no advantage.
predictive_rubric.what_does_support_look_like: In small-scale games where full history fits in context, advanced memory structures show minimal (<5% relative) or no performance improvement over full-history baselines.

predictive_evaluation.evidence_quote_or_locator: Table 1 and Section 4.2. The paper compares LiCoMemory (structured hierarchical graph) against baselines including LoCoMo (chunk-based unstructured retrieval), Memorybank, and MemOS on LongMemEval and LoCoMo benchmarks. Figure 5 ablation study shows 'w/o Structured retrieval' drops performance from 73.8/76.6 to 51.6/56.4, demonstrating that 'When retrieval is performed solely over extracted triples, ignoring session hierarchy and entity-relation structure, performance drops.'
predictive_evaluation.evidence_in_support: LiCoMemory's structured hierarchical graph (CogniGraph) achieves 73.8% accuracy on LongMemEval compared to LoCoMo's unstructured chunk-based approach at 16.6% (Table 1 with GPT-4o-mini). The ablation study explicitly shows that removing structured retrieval causes severe degradation (73.8→51.6% accuracy), with the paper stating this 'indicating fragmented retrieval and weakened factual grounding.' The structured approach consistently outperforms unstructured baselines across all metrics.

predictive_evaluation.evidence_quote_or_locator: Section 3.2 and Table 1. The paper describes selective retrieval using 'hierarchical analysis with an integrated temporally aware re-ranking mechanism' and 'top-down retrieval—from session summaries to triples and then to original chunks.' Table 1 shows LiCoMemory uses 1.7k tokens (selective) vs LoCoMo's 3.5k tokens and Memorybank's 4.1k tokens (brute-force/full context), achieving 73.8% vs 16.6% and 35.4% accuracy respectively.
predictive_evaluation.evidence_in_support: LiCoMemory's selective retrieval mechanism achieves significantly higher accuracy (73.8% on LongMemEval) while using substantially fewer tokens (1.7k) compared to baseline methods that include more context (3.5-4.5k tokens). The paper explicitly states that selective retrieval 'ensures coherent and contextually relevant knowledge selection' and demonstrates better scaling. The system retrieves only top-15 relevant items through semantic similarity and hierarchical structure rather than including full history.

predictive_evaluation.evidence_quote_or_locator: Figure 4 and Section 4.2. The paper evaluates performance across different subset complexities. Specifically, for Multi-Session subset (high complexity): LiCoMemory achieves 26.6% improvement over second-best baseline. For Single-Session subsets (lower complexity): improvements are smaller. The paper states 'LiCoMemory achieves substantial gains on the Multi-Session (26.6%) and Temporal Reasoning (15.9%) subsets... highlighting the effectiveness of our CogniGraph structure.'
predictive_evaluation.evidence_in_support: The performance breakdown in Figure 4 shows that LiCoMemory's advantage increases with task complexity. On complex multi-session tasks, LiCoMemory achieves 26.6% improvement over the second-best baseline, while on simpler single-session tasks the improvements are smaller. On the Temporal-Reasoning subset (another complex task type), the improvement is 19.2% over MemOS. This pattern demonstrates that structured memory's performance gap widens as complexity increases, exactly as predicted.

law.theory_name: Hybrid Structured-Episodic Memory as a Fundamental Requirement for Robust Performance in LLM-Based Text Game Agents
law.theory_description: This theory posits that robust performance, generalization, and strategic flexibility in language model (LLM) agents across complex, partially observable text games require not just large memory but a principled integration of structured (e.g., knowledge/belief graph) and episodic/reflection-based memory. The synergy emerges because structured memory offers compact relational representations for navigation and reasoning, while episodic/reflection memory provides dynamic adaptation, procedural recall, and learning from both failure and success. Neither naive retention of all history nor pure reliance on unstructured episodic memory alone is sufficient for scaling or generalizing to new, difficult, or long-horizon tasks; selective retrieval and composition of these modalities is necessary to balance information compression, retrieval relevance, and depth of reasoning.

law.statement_name: Memory Structure, Selectivity, and Retrieval are as Critical as Memory Size or Modality
law.theory_statement: The benefit of memory augmentation to LLM agents in text games depends not just on the presence or volume of stored experience, but crucially on how memory is structured (graphs, attributes, summaries), accessed (contextual semantic retrieval, attribute filtering, embedding similarity, graph traversal), and composed/interleaved with ongoing reasoning. Unstructured, brute-force context window or naive concatenation yields information overload or sparsity and fails to scale in complex games.
law.domain_scope: LLM-based agents with any form of persistent memory in settings with nontrivial state/action space, partial observability, and long or branching planning horizons.
special_cases: In trivial or small-scale settings where history fits in the prompt, advanced retrieval offers little marginal gain.
In environments with no discoverable structure or pure randomness, selectivity/structure add overhead but little task benefit.

supporting_evidence.text: Worldformer’s graph-diff representation and set-of-sequences (SOS) loss, RAP’s windowed trajectory retrieval, MemInsight’s attribute-based and embedding retrieval, and MC-DML’s distinction between context window and cross-trial memory all yield large performance improvements over naive retrieval or memory-dumping agents.
Ariadne/AriGraph's ablations (full-history, summaries, RAG, Simulacra) and unstructured context concatenation produce much lower performance and poor scaling to complex or long tasks.
ThinkThrice and Sweet&Sour explicitly leverage retrieval, selective reflection, and verification to outperform memoryless or unselective/naive retrieval agents.

GATA, KG-A2C, and MoE agents' use of graph-based masking, attention, and value-seeded mixing demonstrates the criticality of retrieval mechanisms and selective structuring, as opposed to raw memory augmentation.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Importance of retrieval is acknowledged in information retrieval, RAG, and some memory-augmented LM architectures; information overload in full context is a recognized issue.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: This general law formalizes the equivalence (or superiority) of memory structure and access relative to raw memory volume or memory type for performance/goals in complex reasoning environments, based on unified evidence.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Evidence exists in various ablations and isolated system papers, but this theory codifies it at a high level and ties it to both failure and success modes systematically.
references: Borji et al. (2023) A Survey on Large Language Model-Based Game Agents [retrieval cited, not law]
Meng et al. (2025) MemInsight [attribute/embedding retrieval]
Yao et al. (2023) RAG survey and retrieval theory

predictive_rubric.specific_prediction: LLM agents using structured memory representations (e.g., knowledge graphs, belief graphs, attribute-indexed retrieval) outperform agents using unstructured memory (e.g., naive concatenation of full history or raw episodic logs) on complex text games with non-trivial state spaces and partial observability.
operational_signals: Direct comparison between structured memory architectures (graphs, schemas, attributes) and unstructured/concatenation baselines
Performance metrics such as task completion rate, score, success rate, or goal achievement in text games
Ablation studies removing or replacing structured memory with full-history or unstructured approaches
predictive_rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must directly compare a structured memory method against an unstructured or naive concatenation baseline on the same text game environment, reporting quantitative task performance metrics.
predictive_rubric.what_does_support_look_like: Structured memory agents achieve significantly higher task completion rates, scores, or success rates than unstructured/naive concatenation baselines in complex or long-horizon text games.
predictive_rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Unstructured or naive concatenation methods match or exceed the performance of structured memory methods on complex text games with non-trivial state spaces.

predictive_rubric.specific_prediction: LLM agents with selective, contextual memory retrieval mechanisms (e.g., semantic similarity, attribute filtering, graph traversal) outperform agents that use brute-force context inclusion or no retrieval filtering in text games requiring planning and reasoning.
operational_signals: Comparison between selective/contextual retrieval methods and full-history or no-retrieval baselines
Explicit ablation of retrieval mechanisms (e.g., removing semantic retrieval, attribute filtering)
Performance metrics in text games, particularly those with long horizons or large state spaces
predictive_rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must compare an agent with selective retrieval (e.g., embedding-based, attribute-based, or graph-based selection) against a baseline using full context or no retrieval on the same task, with quantitative performance metrics.
predictive_rubric.what_does_support_look_like: Selective retrieval agents demonstrate higher task performance and better scaling to complex or long-horizon games compared to full-history or no-retrieval baselines.
predictive_rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Full-history inclusion or no-retrieval baselines achieve equal or superior performance to selective retrieval methods in complex text games.

predictive_rubric.specific_prediction: LLM agents that combine structured memory (e.g., graphs, schemas) with episodic/reflection-based memory outperform agents relying solely on one memory modality (structured-only or episodic-only) in complex text games.
operational_signals: Ablation studies comparing hybrid memory systems against structured-only or episodic-only variants
Explicit architectural comparison of combined vs. single-modality memory systems
Performance metrics in text games with partial observability and long planning horizons
predictive_rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must compare a hybrid memory architecture (combining structured and episodic/reflection components) against single-modality baselines (structured-only or episodic-only) on the same text game task with quantitative metrics.
predictive_rubric.what_does_support_look_like: Hybrid memory systems achieve higher task completion rates or scores than either structured-only or episodic-only memory systems in complex text games.
predictive_rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Single-modality memory systems (structured-only or episodic-only) match or exceed the performance of hybrid memory architectures in complex text games.

predictive_rubric.specific_prediction: As text game complexity increases (larger state/action spaces, longer horizons, more partial observability), the performance gap between structured/selective memory agents and unstructured/naive memory agents widens in favor of structured/selective approaches.
operational_signals: Performance comparisons across multiple difficulty levels or game complexities
Evaluation on simple vs. complex game variants with varying state space sizes or horizon lengths
Explicit analysis of scaling behavior as task complexity increases
predictive_rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must evaluate both structured/selective and unstructured/naive memory approaches across at least two distinct complexity levels of text games, showing performance trends as complexity increases.
predictive_rubric.what_does_support_look_like: The performance advantage of structured/selective memory over unstructured/naive methods increases as game complexity (state space, horizon, partial observability) increases.
predictive_rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Unstructured/naive memory methods maintain equal performance gaps or close the gap with structured/selective methods as game complexity increases.

predictive_rubric.specific_prediction: In text games where full history fits within the LLM context window and state spaces are small, advanced structured memory and selective retrieval provide minimal or no performance improvement over simple full-history inclusion.
operational_signals: Comparison of advanced memory architectures vs. full-history baselines on small-scale or simple text games

Performance metrics showing marginal differences in low-complexity settings
predictive_rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must compare advanced memory/retrieval methods against full-history baselines on trivial or small-scale text games where history fits in context, reporting performance metrics that show minimal or no advantage.
predictive_rubric.what_does_support_look_like: In small-scale games where full history fits in context, advanced memory structures show minimal (<5% relative) or no performance improvement over full-history baselines.
predictive_rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Advanced memory structures show substantial (>10% relative) performance gains over full-history baselines even in trivial settings where all history fits in context.

predictive_evaluation.evidence_quote_or_locator: Figure 5(c) shows ablation of Memory Distillation. Without distillation (consuming all 60 retrieved memories), the agent achieves F1=34.37, B1=40.95, J=60.14. With distillation (filtering for relevant entries), performance improves to F1=37.51, B1=45.02, J=62.74. The paper states: 'Without distillation, the agent consumes all retrieved memories; with distillation, it filters for the most relevant ones.'
predictive_evaluation.evidence_in_support: The paper directly compares selective retrieval (Memory Distillation) against full-history inclusion (consuming all 60 retrieved memories) in an ablation study. The selective retrieval approach consistently outperforms the brute-force inclusion baseline across all metrics (F1 improvement from 34.37 to 37.51, B1 from 40.95 to 45.02, J from 60.14 to 62.74), demonstrating that filtering irrelevant entries reduces noise and enables more effective reasoning.

law.theory_name: Hybrid Structured-Episodic Memory as a Fundamental Requirement for Robust Performance in LLM-Based Text Game Agents
law.theory_description: This theory posits that robust performance, generalization, and strategic flexibility in language model (LLM) agents across complex, partially observable text games require not just large memory but a principled integration of structured (e.g., knowledge/belief graph) and episodic/reflection-based memory. The synergy emerges because structured memory offers compact relational representations for navigation and reasoning, while episodic/reflection memory provides dynamic adaptation, procedural recall, and learning from both failure and success. Neither naive retention of all history nor pure reliance on unstructured episodic memory alone is sufficient for scaling or generalizing to new, difficult, or long-horizon tasks; selective retrieval and composition of these modalities is necessary to balance information compression, retrieval relevance, and depth of reasoning.

law.statement_name: Memory Structure, Selectivity, and Retrieval are as Critical as Memory Size or Modality
law.theory_statement: The benefit of memory augmentation to LLM agents in text games depends not just on the presence or volume of stored experience, but crucially on how memory is structured (graphs, attributes, summaries), accessed (contextual semantic retrieval, attribute filtering, embedding similarity, graph traversal), and composed/interleaved with ongoing reasoning. Unstructured, brute-force context window or naive concatenation yields information overload or sparsity and fails to scale in complex games.
law.domain_scope: LLM-based agents with any form of persistent memory in settings with nontrivial state/action space, partial observability, and long or branching planning horizons.
special_cases: In trivial or small-scale settings where history fits in the prompt, advanced retrieval offers little marginal gain.
In environments with no discoverable structure or pure randomness, selectivity/structure add overhead but little task benefit.

supporting_evidence.text: Worldformer’s graph-diff representation and set-of-sequences (SOS) loss, RAP’s windowed trajectory retrieval, MemInsight’s attribute-based and embedding retrieval, and MC-DML’s distinction between context window and cross-trial memory all yield large performance improvements over naive retrieval or memory-dumping agents.
Ariadne/AriGraph's ablations (full-history, summaries, RAG, Simulacra) and unstructured context concatenation produce much lower performance and poor scaling to complex or long tasks.
ThinkThrice and Sweet&Sour explicitly leverage retrieval, selective reflection, and verification to outperform memoryless or unselective/naive retrieval agents.

GATA, KG-A2C, and MoE agents' use of graph-based masking, attention, and value-seeded mixing demonstrates the criticality of retrieval mechanisms and selective structuring, as opposed to raw memory augmentation.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Importance of retrieval is acknowledged in information retrieval, RAG, and some memory-augmented LM architectures; information overload in full context is a recognized issue.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: This general law formalizes the equivalence (or superiority) of memory structure and access relative to raw memory volume or memory type for performance/goals in complex reasoning environments, based on unified evidence.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Evidence exists in various ablations and isolated system papers, but this theory codifies it at a high level and ties it to both failure and success modes systematically.
references: Borji et al. (2023) A Survey on Large Language Model-Based Game Agents [retrieval cited, not law]
Meng et al. (2025) MemInsight [attribute/embedding retrieval]
Yao et al. (2023) RAG survey and retrieval theory

predictive_rubric.specific_prediction: LLM agents using structured memory representations (e.g., knowledge graphs, belief graphs, attribute-indexed retrieval) outperform agents using unstructured memory (e.g., naive concatenation of full history or raw episodic logs) on complex text games with non-trivial state spaces and partial observability.
operational_signals: Direct comparison between structured memory architectures (graphs, schemas, attributes) and unstructured/concatenation baselines
Performance metrics such as task completion rate, score, success rate, or goal achievement in text games
Ablation studies removing or replacing structured memory with full-history or unstructured approaches
predictive_rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must directly compare a structured memory method against an unstructured or naive concatenation baseline on the same text game environment, reporting quantitative task performance metrics.
predictive_rubric.what_does_support_look_like: Structured memory agents achieve significantly higher task completion rates, scores, or success rates than unstructured/naive concatenation baselines in complex or long-horizon text games.
predictive_rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Unstructured or naive concatenation methods match or exceed the performance of structured memory methods on complex text games with non-trivial state spaces.

predictive_rubric.specific_prediction: LLM agents with selective, contextual memory retrieval mechanisms (e.g., semantic similarity, attribute filtering, graph traversal) outperform agents that use brute-force context inclusion or no retrieval filtering in text games requiring planning and reasoning.
operational_signals: Comparison between selective/contextual retrieval methods and full-history or no-retrieval baselines
Explicit ablation of retrieval mechanisms (e.g., removing semantic retrieval, attribute filtering)
Performance metrics in text games, particularly those with long horizons or large state spaces
predictive_rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must compare an agent with selective retrieval (e.g., embedding-based, attribute-based, or graph-based selection) against a baseline using full context or no retrieval on the same task, with quantitative performance metrics.
predictive_rubric.what_does_support_look_like: Selective retrieval agents demonstrate higher task performance and better scaling to complex or long-horizon games compared to full-history or no-retrieval baselines.
predictive_rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Full-history inclusion or no-retrieval baselines achieve equal or superior performance to selective retrieval methods in complex text games.

predictive_rubric.specific_prediction: LLM agents that combine structured memory (e.g., graphs, schemas) with episodic/reflection-based memory outperform agents relying solely on one memory modality (structured-only or episodic-only) in complex text games.
operational_signals: Ablation studies comparing hybrid memory systems against structured-only or episodic-only variants
Explicit architectural comparison of combined vs. single-modality memory systems
Performance metrics in text games with partial observability and long planning horizons
predictive_rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must compare a hybrid memory architecture (combining structured and episodic/reflection components) against single-modality baselines (structured-only or episodic-only) on the same text game task with quantitative metrics.
predictive_rubric.what_does_support_look_like: Hybrid memory systems achieve higher task completion rates or scores than either structured-only or episodic-only memory systems in complex text games.
predictive_rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Single-modality memory systems (structured-only or episodic-only) match or exceed the performance of hybrid memory architectures in complex text games.

predictive_rubric.specific_prediction: As text game complexity increases (larger state/action spaces, longer horizons, more partial observability), the performance gap between structured/selective memory agents and unstructured/naive memory agents widens in favor of structured/selective approaches.
operational_signals: Performance comparisons across multiple difficulty levels or game complexities
Evaluation on simple vs. complex game variants with varying state space sizes or horizon lengths
Explicit analysis of scaling behavior as task complexity increases
predictive_rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must evaluate both structured/selective and unstructured/naive memory approaches across at least two distinct complexity levels of text games, showing performance trends as complexity increases.
predictive_rubric.what_does_support_look_like: The performance advantage of structured/selective memory over unstructured/naive methods increases as game complexity (state space, horizon, partial observability) increases.
predictive_rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Unstructured/naive memory methods maintain equal performance gaps or close the gap with structured/selective methods as game complexity increases.

predictive_rubric.specific_prediction: In text games where full history fits within the LLM context window and state spaces are small, advanced structured memory and selective retrieval provide minimal or no performance improvement over simple full-history inclusion.
operational_signals: Comparison of advanced memory architectures vs. full-history baselines on small-scale or simple text games
Explicit evaluation on tasks where context limits are not reached
Performance metrics showing marginal differences in low-complexity settings
predictive_rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must compare advanced memory/retrieval methods against full-history baselines on trivial or small-scale text games where history fits in context, reporting performance metrics that show minimal or no advantage.
predictive_rubric.what_does_support_look_like: In small-scale games where full history fits in context, advanced memory structures show minimal (<5% relative) or no performance improvement over full-history baselines.
predictive_rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Advanced memory structures show substantial (>10% relative) performance gains over full-history baselines even in trivial settings where all history fits in context.

predictive_evaluation.evidence_quote_or_locator: Section 3.3 describes proactive retrieval of Procedural Memory SOPs, and Table 3 shows ablation without Reflect Agent, but no direct comparison of selective retrieval vs. full-history/no-retrieval baselines on the same task.

Section 4.3.3 and Table 2 show performance on full TAC benchmark vs. continuous learning subset T_cl, but this compares memory-equipped vs. memory-less agents across different task sets, not structured vs. unstructured memory approaches across complexity levels.
