predictive_evaluation.evidence_quote_or_locator: Table 2 and Table 4 compare Full Context (unstructured naive concatenation) against RAG and A-Mem (structured memory with embeddings, attributes, tags). Section 4.1 and 4.3 report quantitative F1 scores across reasoning categories. For example, GPT-4o mini with RAG achieves 30.06 F1 on Multi-Hop vs 20.40 with Full Context; Qwen 7B with RAG achieves 23.14 vs 12.32 Full Context on Multi-Hop.
predictive_evaluation.evidence_in_support: The paper directly compares structured memory approaches (RAG with semantic retrieval, A-Mem with attribute-indexed retrieval and contextual tags) against Full Context baseline (naive concatenation of full history) on the LoCoMo text game dataset. Tables 2 and 4 show that structured approaches like RAG and A-Mem achieve competitive or superior performance to Full Context on most reasoning categories while reducing token usage by over 90%. Section 4.3 explicitly states that RAG substantially outperforms Full Context across foundation models.

predictive_evaluation.evidence_quote_or_locator: Table 2 compares RAG (selective top-k retrieval via semantic similarity) against Full Context (brute-force inclusion of all history). Section 4.1 reports F1 scores and token counts. Table 5 in Appendix A shows ablation of k values (5, 10, 20) for retrieval selectivity. Section 4.6 reports token efficiency: RAG uses 600-1500 tokens vs 23000+ for Full Context.
predictive_evaluation.evidence_in_support: The paper directly compares selective retrieval (RAG with top-k semantic similarity retrieval) against full-history inclusion (Full Context baseline) on the same LoCoMo tasks. Table 2 shows RAG achieves competitive or better performance than Full Context while reducing token usage by over 90% (Section 4.6). For example, GPT-4o mini with RAG uses 649 tokens vs 23132 for Full Context, while achieving 30.06 vs 20.40 F1 on Multi-Hop reasoning. This demonstrates selective retrieval outperforms brute-force context inclusion in both efficiency and often in accuracy.

predictive_evaluation.evidence_quote_or_locator: Table 2 compares RAG (semantic-only), RAG+EpMem (hybrid semantic+episodic), and RAG+PromptOpt+EpMem (semantic+episodic+procedural). Section 4.4 reports: 'RAG+EpMem outperforms RAG on GPT-4o mini and Llama3.2 3B Instruct (respectively, 1.30 vs. 3.20 and 1.83 vs. 4.5 F1 ranking).' Table 2 shows GPT-4o mini RAG+EpMem achieves average ranking 1.66 vs RAG 2.66.
predictive_evaluation.evidence_in_support: The paper explicitly compares hybrid memory (RAG+EpMem combining semantic and episodic memory) against single-modality RAG (semantic-only) on LoCoMo. Table 2 shows RAG+EpMem achieves better average F1 rankings than RAG alone for GPT-4o mini (1.66 vs 2.66) and Llama 3B Instruct (1.83 vs 4.50). Section 4.4 states improvements are seen in adversarial, temporal, and multi-hop reasoning categories, demonstrating that combining structured semantic memory with episodic reflections outperforms semantic memory alone.

predictive_evaluation.evidence_quote_or_locator: Section 4.1 mentions different reasoning categories (Single-Hop, Multi-Hop, Temporal, Open-Domain, Adversarial) which represent varying complexity levels. However, the paper does not systematically evaluate the same approaches across explicitly varied complexity levels of the same game type (e.g., simple vs. complex variants with different state space sizes).

Table 3 shows results on QMSum dataset which has shorter contexts (13k tokens average vs 23k for LoCoMo). Full Context achieves best performance for GPT-4o mini (27.70 vs 20.03 RAG), suggesting simpler settings may not benefit from advanced memory structures. However, the paper does not explicitly test trivial/small-scale games where history fits easily in context.

law.theory_name: Hybrid Structured-Episodic Memory as a Fundamental Requirement for Robust Performance in LLM-Based Text Game Agents
law.theory_description: This theory posits that robust performance, generalization, and strategic flexibility in language model (LLM) agents across complex, partially observable text games require not just large memory but a principled integration of structured (e.g., knowledge/belief graph) and episodic/reflection-based memory. The synergy emerges because structured memory offers compact relational representations for navigation and reasoning, while episodic/reflection memory provides dynamic adaptation, procedural recall, and learning from both failure and success. Neither naive retention of all history nor pure reliance on unstructured episodic memory alone is sufficient for scaling or generalizing to new, difficult, or long-horizon tasks; selective retrieval and composition of these modalities is necessary to balance information compression, retrieval relevance, and depth of reasoning.

law.statement_name: Memory Structure, Selectivity, and Retrieval are as Critical as Memory Size or Modality
law.theory_statement: The benefit of memory augmentation to LLM agents in text games depends not just on the presence or volume of stored experience, but crucially on how memory is structured (graphs, attributes, summaries), accessed (contextual semantic retrieval, attribute filtering, embedding similarity, graph traversal), and composed/interleaved with ongoing reasoning. Unstructured, brute-force context window or naive concatenation yields information overload or sparsity and fails to scale in complex games.
law.domain_scope: LLM-based agents with any form of persistent memory in settings with nontrivial state/action space, partial observability, and long or branching planning horizons.
special_cases: In trivial or small-scale settings where history fits in the prompt, advanced retrieval offers little marginal gain.
In environments with no discoverable structure or pure randomness, selectivity/structure add overhead but little task benefit.

supporting_evidence.text: Worldformer’s graph-diff representation and set-of-sequences (SOS) loss, RAP’s windowed trajectory retrieval, MemInsight’s attribute-based and embedding retrieval, and MC-DML’s distinction between context window and cross-trial memory all yield large performance improvements over naive retrieval or memory-dumping agents.
Ariadne/AriGraph's ablations (full-history, summaries, RAG, Simulacra) and unstructured context concatenation produce much lower performance and poor scaling to complex or long tasks.
ThinkThrice and Sweet&Sour explicitly leverage retrieval, selective reflection, and verification to outperform memoryless or unselective/naive retrieval agents.

GATA, KG-A2C, and MoE agents' use of graph-based masking, attention, and value-seeded mixing demonstrates the criticality of retrieval mechanisms and selective structuring, as opposed to raw memory augmentation.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Importance of retrieval is acknowledged in information retrieval, RAG, and some memory-augmented LM architectures; information overload in full context is a recognized issue.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: This general law formalizes the equivalence (or superiority) of memory structure and access relative to raw memory volume or memory type for performance/goals in complex reasoning environments, based on unified evidence.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Evidence exists in various ablations and isolated system papers, but this theory codifies it at a high level and ties it to both failure and success modes systematically.
references: Borji et al. (2023) A Survey on Large Language Model-Based Game Agents [retrieval cited, not law]
Meng et al. (2025) MemInsight [attribute/embedding retrieval]
Yao et al. (2023) RAG survey and retrieval theory

predictive_evaluation.evidence_quote_or_locator: Table I and Table II compare Memoria (using selective semantic retrieval with top-K=20 and exponential decay weighting) against Full Context (brute-force inclusion of entire 115K token conversation history). Table I shows Memoria achieves 87.1% vs 85.7% on Single-Session and 80.8% vs 78.2% on Knowledge-Update. Table II shows Memoria reduces inference time by 38.7% (260 vs 391 seconds, 320 vs 522 seconds) and token length from 115K to ~400 tokens.
predictive_evaluation.evidence_in_support: Memoria's selective retrieval with semantic similarity and recency weighting outperforms full-context baseline on both accuracy metrics (Table I: 87.1% vs 85.7%, 80.8% vs 78.2%) and achieves substantial efficiency gains (Table II: 38.7% latency reduction, 115K to 400 tokens). This directly demonstrates that selective retrieval mechanisms outperform brute-force context inclusion in the conversational AI task evaluated.

law.theory_name: Hybrid Structured-Episodic Memory as a Fundamental Requirement for Robust Performance in LLM-Based Text Game Agents
law.theory_description: This theory posits that robust performance, generalization, and strategic flexibility in language model (LLM) agents across complex, partially observable text games require not just large memory but a principled integration of structured (e.g., knowledge/belief graph) and episodic/reflection-based memory. The synergy emerges because structured memory offers compact relational representations for navigation and reasoning, while episodic/reflection memory provides dynamic adaptation, procedural recall, and learning from both failure and success. Neither naive retention of all history nor pure reliance on unstructured episodic memory alone is sufficient for scaling or generalizing to new, difficult, or long-horizon tasks; selective retrieval and composition of these modalities is necessary to balance information compression, retrieval relevance, and depth of reasoning.

law.statement_name: Memory Structure, Selectivity, and Retrieval are as Critical as Memory Size or Modality
law.theory_statement: The benefit of memory augmentation to LLM agents in text games depends not just on the presence or volume of stored experience, but crucially on how memory is structured (graphs, attributes, summaries), accessed (contextual semantic retrieval, attribute filtering, embedding similarity, graph traversal), and composed/interleaved with ongoing reasoning. Unstructured, brute-force context window or naive concatenation yields information overload or sparsity and fails to scale in complex games.
law.domain_scope: LLM-based agents with any form of persistent memory in settings with nontrivial state/action space, partial observability, and long or branching planning horizons.
special_cases: In trivial or small-scale settings where history fits in the prompt, advanced retrieval offers little marginal gain.
In environments with no discoverable structure or pure randomness, selectivity/structure add overhead but little task benefit.

supporting_evidence.text: Worldformer’s graph-diff representation and set-of-sequences (SOS) loss, RAP’s windowed trajectory retrieval, MemInsight’s attribute-based and embedding retrieval, and MC-DML’s distinction between context window and cross-trial memory all yield large performance improvements over naive retrieval or memory-dumping agents.
Ariadne/AriGraph's ablations (full-history, summaries, RAG, Simulacra) and unstructured context concatenation produce much lower performance and poor scaling to complex or long tasks.
ThinkThrice and Sweet&Sour explicitly leverage retrieval, selective reflection, and verification to outperform memoryless or unselective/naive retrieval agents.

GATA, KG-A2C, and MoE agents' use of graph-based masking, attention, and value-seeded mixing demonstrates the criticality of retrieval mechanisms and selective structuring, as opposed to raw memory augmentation.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Importance of retrieval is acknowledged in information retrieval, RAG, and some memory-augmented LM architectures; information overload in full context is a recognized issue.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: This general law formalizes the equivalence (or superiority) of memory structure and access relative to raw memory volume or memory type for performance/goals in complex reasoning environments, based on unified evidence.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Evidence exists in various ablations and isolated system papers, but this theory codifies it at a high level and ties it to both failure and success modes systematically.
references: Borji et al. (2023) A Survey on Large Language Model-Based Game Agents [retrieval cited, not law]
Meng et al. (2025) MemInsight [attribute/embedding retrieval]
Yao et al. (2023) RAG survey and retrieval theory

predictive_rubric.specific_prediction: LLM agents using structured memory representations (e.g., knowledge graphs, belief graphs, attribute-indexed retrieval) outperform agents using unstructured memory (e.g., naive concatenation of full history or raw episodic logs) on complex text games with non-trivial state spaces and partial observability.
operational_signals: Direct comparison between structured memory architectures (graphs, schemas, attributes) and unstructured/concatenation baselines
Performance metrics such as task completion rate, score, success rate, or goal achievement in text games
Ablation studies removing or replacing structured memory with full-history or unstructured approaches
predictive_rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must directly compare a structured memory method against an unstructured or naive concatenation baseline on the same text game environment, reporting quantitative task performance metrics.
predictive_rubric.what_does_support_look_like: Structured memory agents achieve significantly higher task completion rates, scores, or success rates than unstructured/naive concatenation baselines in complex or long-horizon text games.
predictive_rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Unstructured or naive concatenation methods match or exceed the performance of structured memory methods on complex text games with non-trivial state spaces.

predictive_rubric.specific_prediction: LLM agents with selective, contextual memory retrieval mechanisms (e.g., semantic similarity, attribute filtering, graph traversal) outperform agents that use brute-force context inclusion or no retrieval filtering in text games requiring planning and reasoning.
operational_signals: Comparison between selective/contextual retrieval methods and full-history or no-retrieval baselines
Explicit ablation of retrieval mechanisms (e.g., removing semantic retrieval, attribute filtering)
Performance metrics in text games, particularly those with long horizons or large state spaces
predictive_rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must compare an agent with selective retrieval (e.g., embedding-based, attribute-based, or graph-based selection) against a baseline using full context or no retrieval on the same task, with quantitative performance metrics.
predictive_rubric.what_does_support_look_like: Selective retrieval agents demonstrate higher task performance and better scaling to complex or long-horizon games compared to full-history or no-retrieval baselines.
predictive_rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Full-history inclusion or no-retrieval baselines achieve equal or superior performance to selective retrieval methods in complex text games.

predictive_rubric.specific_prediction: LLM agents that combine structured memory (e.g., graphs, schemas) with episodic/reflection-based memory outperform agents relying solely on one memory modality (structured-only or episodic-only) in complex text games.
operational_signals: Ablation studies comparing hybrid memory systems against structured-only or episodic-only variants
Explicit architectural comparison of combined vs. single-modality memory systems
Performance metrics in text games with partial observability and long planning horizons
predictive_rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must compare a hybrid memory architecture (combining structured and episodic/reflection components) against single-modality baselines (structured-only or episodic-only) on the same text game task with quantitative metrics.
predictive_rubric.what_does_support_look_like: Hybrid memory systems achieve higher task completion rates or scores than either structured-only or episodic-only memory systems in complex text games.
predictive_rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Single-modality memory systems (structured-only or episodic-only) match or exceed the performance of hybrid memory architectures in complex text games.

predictive_rubric.specific_prediction: As text game complexity increases (larger state/action spaces, longer horizons, more partial observability), the performance gap between structured/selective memory agents and unstructured/naive memory agents widens in favor of structured/selective approaches.
operational_signals: Performance comparisons across multiple difficulty levels or game complexities
Evaluation on simple vs. complex game variants with varying state space sizes or horizon lengths
Explicit analysis of scaling behavior as task complexity increases
predictive_rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must evaluate both structured/selective and unstructured/naive memory approaches across at least two distinct complexity levels of text games, showing performance trends as complexity increases.
predictive_rubric.what_does_support_look_like: The performance advantage of structured/selective memory over unstructured/naive methods increases as game complexity (state space, horizon, partial observability) increases.
predictive_rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Unstructured/naive memory methods maintain equal performance gaps or close the gap with structured/selective methods as game complexity increases.

predictive_rubric.specific_prediction: In text games where full history fits within the LLM context window and state spaces are small, advanced structured memory and selective retrieval provide minimal or no performance improvement over simple full-history inclusion.
operational_signals: Comparison of advanced memory architectures vs. full-history baselines on small-scale or simple text games
Explicit evaluation on tasks where context limits are not reached
Performance metrics showing marginal differences in low-complexity settings
predictive_rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must compare advanced memory/retrieval methods against full-history baselines on trivial or small-scale text games where history fits in context, reporting performance metrics that show minimal or no advantage.
predictive_rubric.what_does_support_look_like: In small-scale games where full history fits in context, advanced memory structures show minimal (<5% relative) or no performance improvement over full-history baselines.
predictive_rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Advanced memory structures show substantial (>10% relative) performance gains over full-history baselines even in trivial settings where all history fits in context.

predictive_evaluation.evidence_quote_or_locator: Figure 2, Table 4, and Section 4.1. The paper compares Intrinsic Memory (structured, agent-specific memory) against multiple baselines including 'Empty' (no memory) and other memory mechanisms across PDDL, FEVER, and ALFWorld benchmarks. Quantitative performance metrics (mean rewards and standard deviations) are reported.
predictive_evaluation.evidence_in_support: The Intrinsic Memory approach with structured, agent-specific memory templates outperforms all other memory mechanisms on PDDL (0.260 vs 0.224 for Empty baseline, p<0.05), shows competitive performance on FEVER (0.653 vs 0.646 for Empty), and demonstrates consistent performance on ALFWorld (0.048 vs 0.020 for Empty). The paper explicitly states that 'agent-specific memories aligned with conversational objectives' maintain 'both historical context and recent developments while preserving agent-specific perspectives.'

predictive_evaluation.evidence_quote_or_locator: Section 3.2 and Algorithm 1 describe a context construction function that prioritizes memory and recent turns, but there is no direct ablation comparing selective retrieval mechanisms (embedding-based, attribute filtering) against full-history inclusion.

Table 4 and Section 4.1 show performance across three different benchmarks (PDDL, FEVER, ALFWorld) which vary in complexity, but the paper does not explicitly analyze scaling behavior or performance gaps as complexity increases.

predictive_evaluation.evidence_quote_or_locator: Table 1 and Table 2 show PISA (structured memory with schema-based organization) compared against multiple baselines including MemoryOS, Zep, AMem, Mem0, and LangMem on LOCOMO and AggQA benchmarks. The paper states 'PISA introduces a trimodal adaptation mechanism (i.e., schema updation, schema evolution, and schema creation) that preserves coherent organization' and describes its Schema Engine as hierarchical structured memory.
predictive_evaluation.evidence_in_support: PISA, which uses structured memory (Schema Engine with hierarchical organization into Memory Pool, Buckets, and Schemas), achieves 63.58% average accuracy on LOCOMO compared to baselines (34.10-55.51%), and 83.67% (Med.) and 69.41% (Fin.) on AggQA compared to baselines (10.20-51.02% Med., 9.41-30.59% Fin.). The paper explicitly states that structured memory with schema-based organization outperforms other memory systems.

predictive_evaluation.evidence_quote_or_locator: Table 3 shows ablation study PISA^{-R} on Fin. dataset where the retrieval module is 'blinded' to the memory's hierarchical structure. The paper states: 'we evaluated the Retrieval Module by 'blinding' it to the memory's hierarchical structure on the Fin dataset (PISA^{-R}). This led to a 17.64% decrease in overall performance, with a severe drop on hard tasks (from 78.57% to 50.00%).' Section 3.4 describes the Retrieval Module with task-aware retrieval strategies.
predictive_evaluation.evidence_in_support: The ablation study shows that removing selective, structure-aware retrieval (PISA^{-R}) causes performance to drop from 69.41% to 51.77% overall on the Finance domain of AggQA, with especially severe drops on hard tasks (78.57% to 50.00%). The paper explicitly states this demonstrates 'that structural awareness is vital for the agent to efficiently orchestrate tools and solve complex analytical queries.'

predictive_evaluation.evidence_quote_or_locator: Table 2 shows performance across Easy, Medium, and Hard difficulty levels on AggQA benchmark. PISA shows 90.91% (Easy), 96.00% (Medium), 53.85% (Hard) on Medical domain, and 78.26% (Easy), 62.50% (Medium), 78.57% (Hard) on Finance domain.

law.theory_name: Hybrid Structured-Episodic Memory as a Fundamental Requirement for Robust Performance in LLM-Based Text Game Agents
law.theory_description: This theory posits that robust performance, generalization, and strategic flexibility in language model (LLM) agents across complex, partially observable text games require not just large memory but a principled integration of structured (e.g., knowledge/belief graph) and episodic/reflection-based memory. The synergy emerges because structured memory offers compact relational representations for navigation and reasoning, while episodic/reflection memory provides dynamic adaptation, procedural recall, and learning from both failure and success. Neither naive retention of all history nor pure reliance on unstructured episodic memory alone is sufficient for scaling or generalizing to new, difficult, or long-horizon tasks; selective retrieval and composition of these modalities is necessary to balance information compression, retrieval relevance, and depth of reasoning.

law.statement_name: Memory Structure, Selectivity, and Retrieval are as Critical as Memory Size or Modality
law.theory_statement: The benefit of memory augmentation to LLM agents in text games depends not just on the presence or volume of stored experience, but crucially on how memory is structured (graphs, attributes, summaries), accessed (contextual semantic retrieval, attribute filtering, embedding similarity, graph traversal), and composed/interleaved with ongoing reasoning. Unstructured, brute-force context window or naive concatenation yields information overload or sparsity and fails to scale in complex games.
law.domain_scope: LLM-based agents with any form of persistent memory in settings with nontrivial state/action space, partial observability, and long or branching planning horizons.
special_cases: In trivial or small-scale settings where history fits in the prompt, advanced retrieval offers little marginal gain.
In environments with no discoverable structure or pure randomness, selectivity/structure add overhead but little task benefit.

supporting_evidence.text: Worldformer’s graph-diff representation and set-of-sequences (SOS) loss, RAP’s windowed trajectory retrieval, MemInsight’s attribute-based and embedding retrieval, and MC-DML’s distinction between context window and cross-trial memory all yield large performance improvements over naive retrieval or memory-dumping agents.
Ariadne/AriGraph's ablations (full-history, summaries, RAG, Simulacra) and unstructured context concatenation produce much lower performance and poor scaling to complex or long tasks.
ThinkThrice and Sweet&Sour explicitly leverage retrieval, selective reflection, and verification to outperform memoryless or unselective/naive retrieval agents.

GATA, KG-A2C, and MoE agents' use of graph-based masking, attention, and value-seeded mixing demonstrates the criticality of retrieval mechanisms and selective structuring, as opposed to raw memory augmentation.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Importance of retrieval is acknowledged in information retrieval, RAG, and some memory-augmented LM architectures; information overload in full context is a recognized issue.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: This general law formalizes the equivalence (or superiority) of memory structure and access relative to raw memory volume or memory type for performance/goals in complex reasoning environments, based on unified evidence.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Evidence exists in various ablations and isolated system papers, but this theory codifies it at a high level and ties it to both failure and success modes systematically.
references: Borji et al. (2023) A Survey on Large Language Model-Based Game Agents [retrieval cited, not law]
Meng et al. (2025) MemInsight [attribute/embedding retrieval]
Yao et al. (2023) RAG survey and retrieval theory

predictive_evaluation.evidence_quote_or_locator: Table 3 (LongMemEval results) comparing Full-context baselines against Hindsight with structured four-network memory organization. Section 7.4 states: 'HINDSIGHT with OSS-20B achieves 83.6% overall accuracy, a +44.6 point gain over the Full-context OSS-20B baseline (39.0%)'
predictive_evaluation.evidence_in_support: The paper directly compares structured memory (Hindsight's four-network architecture with world, experience, opinion, and observation networks) against unstructured full-context baselines. On LongMemEval, Hindsight with OSS-20B achieves 83.6% vs 39.0% for full-context OSS-20B baseline, and even outperforms full-context GPT-4o (60.2%). Similar gains are shown on LoCoMo where Hindsight consistently outperforms baselines across all metrics.

predictive_evaluation.evidence_quote_or_locator: Section 4.2 describes four-way parallel retrieval (semantic, keyword, graph, temporal) with RRF fusion and cross-encoder reranking. Table 3 and 4 compare this selective retrieval against full-context baselines. Section 7.4: 'multi-session questions improve from 21.1% to 79.7% and temporal reasoning from 31.6% to 79.7%'
predictive_evaluation.evidence_in_support: The paper implements selective retrieval through multi-strategy search (semantic vector search, BM25 keyword search, graph traversal, temporal filtering) with fusion and neural reranking, and compares this against full-context inclusion baselines. Results show substantial improvements: on LongMemEval, multi-session accuracy increases from 21.1% to 79.7%, temporal reasoning from 31.6% to 79.7%, demonstrating that selective retrieval outperforms brute-force context inclusion.

predictive_evaluation.evidence_quote_or_locator: Section 3.1 describes the four-network organization (world, experience, opinion, observation), but no ablation comparing hybrid vs single-modality systems is provided.

Section 7.1.1 describes LongMemEval S setting (~115k tokens, 50 sessions) and M setting (~1.5M tokens, 500 sessions). Section 7.4 states: 'The largest gains over the Full-context OSS-20B baseline appear exactly in the long-horizon categories LongMemEval was designed to stress: multi-session questions improve from 21.1% to 79.7% and temporal reasoning from 31.6% to 79.7%'
predictive_evaluation.evidence_in_support: The paper evaluates on two complexity levels: LongMemEval S (~115k tokens, 50 sessions) and M (~1.5M tokens, 500 sessions). Results show that structured memory's advantage is largest in long-horizon categories (multi-session: +58.6 points, temporal reasoning: +48.1 points), demonstrating that the performance gap widens as complexity increases. The paper explicitly notes these are the categories 'LongMemEval was designed to stress' where context dilution is most problematic.

Section 5.2 reports: 'On the LongMemEval benchmark, LightMem consistently outperforms...improving accuracy by 2.09%-6.40% with GPT and up to 7.67% with Qwen' compared to A-MEM, and substantially outperforms Full Text and Naive RAG.
predictive_evaluation.evidence_in_support: The paper directly compares LightMem's structured memory architecture (with topic-aware segmentation, sensory memory filtering, and hierarchical short-term/long-term memory) against unstructured baselines including Full Text (naive concatenation) and Naive RAG. Tables 2 and 3 demonstrate that LightMem achieves 68.64% accuracy vs. 56.80% for Full Text and 61.00% for Naive RAG on LongMemEval with GPT-4o-mini. Similar improvements are shown across both benchmarks and both model backbones, with structured memory consistently outperforming unstructured approaches in complex text-based conversational scenarios.

law.theory_name: Hybrid Structured-Episodic Memory as a Fundamental Requirement for Robust Performance in LLM-Based Text Game Agents
law.theory_description: This theory posits that robust performance, generalization, and strategic flexibility in language model (LLM) agents across complex, partially observable text games require not just large memory but a principled integration of structured (e.g., knowledge/belief graph) and episodic/reflection-based memory. The synergy emerges because structured memory offers compact relational representations for navigation and reasoning, while episodic/reflection memory provides dynamic adaptation, procedural recall, and learning from both failure and success. Neither naive retention of all history nor pure reliance on unstructured episodic memory alone is sufficient for scaling or generalizing to new, difficult, or long-horizon tasks; selective retrieval and composition of these modalities is necessary to balance information compression, retrieval relevance, and depth of reasoning.

law.statement_name: Memory Structure, Selectivity, and Retrieval are as Critical as Memory Size or Modality
law.theory_statement: The benefit of memory augmentation to LLM agents in text games depends not just on the presence or volume of stored experience, but crucially on how memory is structured (graphs, attributes, summaries), accessed (contextual semantic retrieval, attribute filtering, embedding similarity, graph traversal), and composed/interleaved with ongoing reasoning. Unstructured, brute-force context window or naive concatenation yields information overload or sparsity and fails to scale in complex games.
law.domain_scope: LLM-based agents with any form of persistent memory in settings with nontrivial state/action space, partial observability, and long or branching planning horizons.
special_cases: In trivial or small-scale settings where history fits in the prompt, advanced retrieval offers little marginal gain.
In environments with no discoverable structure or pure randomness, selectivity/structure add overhead but little task benefit.

supporting_evidence.text: Worldformer’s graph-diff representation and set-of-sequences (SOS) loss, RAP’s windowed trajectory retrieval, MemInsight’s attribute-based and embedding retrieval, and MC-DML’s distinction between context window and cross-trial memory all yield large performance improvements over naive retrieval or memory-dumping agents.
Ariadne/AriGraph's ablations (full-history, summaries, RAG, Simulacra) and unstructured context concatenation produce much lower performance and poor scaling to complex or long tasks.
ThinkThrice and Sweet&Sour explicitly leverage retrieval, selective reflection, and verification to outperform memoryless or unselective/naive retrieval agents.

GATA, KG-A2C, and MoE agents' use of graph-based masking, attention, and value-seeded mixing demonstrates the criticality of retrieval mechanisms and selective structuring, as opposed to raw memory augmentation.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Importance of retrieval is acknowledged in information retrieval, RAG, and some memory-augmented LM architectures; information overload in full context is a recognized issue.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: This general law formalizes the equivalence (or superiority) of memory structure and access relative to raw memory volume or memory type for performance/goals in complex reasoning environments, based on unified evidence.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Evidence exists in various ablations and isolated system papers, but this theory codifies it at a high level and ties it to both failure and success modes systematically.
references: Borji et al. (2023) A Survey on Large Language Model-Based Game Agents [retrieval cited, not law]
Meng et al. (2025) MemInsight [attribute/embedding retrieval]
Yao et al. (2023) RAG survey and retrieval theory

predictive_rubric.specific_prediction: LLM agents using structured memory representations (e.g., knowledge graphs, belief graphs, attribute-indexed retrieval) outperform agents using unstructured memory (e.g., naive concatenation of full history or raw episodic logs) on complex text games with non-trivial state spaces and partial observability.
operational_signals: Direct comparison between structured memory architectures (graphs, schemas, attributes) and unstructured/concatenation baselines
Performance metrics such as task completion rate, score, success rate, or goal achievement in text games
Ablation studies removing or replacing structured memory with full-history or unstructured approaches
predictive_rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must directly compare a structured memory method against an unstructured or naive concatenation baseline on the same text game environment, reporting quantitative task performance metrics.
predictive_rubric.what_does_support_look_like: Structured memory agents achieve significantly higher task completion rates, scores, or success rates than unstructured/naive concatenation baselines in complex or long-horizon text games.
predictive_rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Unstructured or naive concatenation methods match or exceed the performance of structured memory methods on complex text games with non-trivial state spaces.

predictive_rubric.specific_prediction: LLM agents with selective, contextual memory retrieval mechanisms (e.g., semantic similarity, attribute filtering, graph traversal) outperform agents that use brute-force context inclusion or no retrieval filtering in text games requiring planning and reasoning.
operational_signals: Comparison between selective/contextual retrieval methods and full-history or no-retrieval baselines
Explicit ablation of retrieval mechanisms (e.g., removing semantic retrieval, attribute filtering)
Performance metrics in text games, particularly those with long horizons or large state spaces
predictive_rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must compare an agent with selective retrieval (e.g., embedding-based, attribute-based, or graph-based selection) against a baseline using full context or no retrieval on the same task, with quantitative performance metrics.
predictive_rubric.what_does_support_look_like: Selective retrieval agents demonstrate higher task performance and better scaling to complex or long-horizon games compared to full-history or no-retrieval baselines.
predictive_rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Full-history inclusion or no-retrieval baselines achieve equal or superior performance to selective retrieval methods in complex text games.

predictive_rubric.specific_prediction: LLM agents that combine structured memory (e.g., graphs, schemas) with episodic/reflection-based memory outperform agents relying solely on one memory modality (structured-only or episodic-only) in complex text games.
operational_signals: Ablation studies comparing hybrid memory systems against structured-only or episodic-only variants
Explicit architectural comparison of combined vs. single-modality memory systems
Performance metrics in text games with partial observability and long planning horizons
predictive_rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must compare a hybrid memory architecture (combining structured and episodic/reflection components) against single-modality baselines (structured-only or episodic-only) on the same text game task with quantitative metrics.
predictive_rubric.what_does_support_look_like: Hybrid memory systems achieve higher task completion rates or scores than either structured-only or episodic-only memory systems in complex text games.
predictive_rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Single-modality memory systems (structured-only or episodic-only) match or exceed the performance of hybrid memory architectures in complex text games.

predictive_rubric.specific_prediction: As text game complexity increases (larger state/action spaces, longer horizons, more partial observability), the performance gap between structured/selective memory agents and unstructured/naive memory agents widens in favor of structured/selective approaches.
operational_signals: Performance comparisons across multiple difficulty levels or game complexities
Evaluation on simple vs. complex game variants with varying state space sizes or horizon lengths
Explicit analysis of scaling behavior as task complexity increases
predictive_rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must evaluate both structured/selective and unstructured/naive memory approaches across at least two distinct complexity levels of text games, showing performance trends as complexity increases.
predictive_rubric.what_does_support_look_like: The performance advantage of structured/selective memory over unstructured/naive methods increases as game complexity (state space, horizon, partial observability) increases.
predictive_rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Unstructured/naive memory methods maintain equal performance gaps or close the gap with structured/selective methods as game complexity increases.

predictive_rubric.specific_prediction: In text games where full history fits within the LLM context window and state spaces are small, advanced structured memory and selective retrieval provide minimal or no performance improvement over simple full-history inclusion.
operational_signals: Comparison of advanced memory architectures vs. full-history baselines on small-scale or simple text games
Explicit evaluation on tasks where context limits are not reached
Performance metrics showing marginal differences in low-complexity settings
predictive_rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must compare advanced memory/retrieval methods against full-history baselines on trivial or small-scale text games where history fits in context, reporting performance metrics that show minimal or no advantage.
predictive_rubric.what_does_support_look_like: In small-scale games where full history fits in context, advanced memory structures show minimal (<5% relative) or no performance improvement over full-history baselines.
predictive_rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Advanced memory structures show substantial (>10% relative) performance gains over full-history baselines even in trivial settings where all history fits in context.

predictive_evaluation.evidence_quote_or_locator: Table 2, Section 5.2. The paper compares 'Qwen2.5-VL-7B + Text-based Memory' (unstructured concatenation) vs 'Qwen2.5-VL-7B + CoMEM' (continuous structured embeddings) across MMInA, Mind2Web, and WebVoyager benchmarks.
predictive_evaluation.evidence_in_support: CoMEM consistently and substantially outperforms text-based memory across all benchmarks. For Qwen2.5-VL-7B, CoMEM achieves 47.4% vs 34.2% on MMInA Wikipedia, 45.0% vs 31.4% on MMInA Shopping, 22.2% vs 7.1% on Mind2Web Shopping, and 54.5% vs 44.0% on WebVoyager. The paper explicitly states that text-based memories 'degrade beyond roughly ten retrieved items due to ballooning sequence length' while CoMEM shows 'monotonic gains as memory size and retrieved items scale.'

predictive_evaluation.evidence_quote_or_locator: Section 4.2 describes 'embedding-based multimodal retrieval' with CLIP encoder and FAISS indexing for top-k retrieval. Figure 1(b) shows performance scaling with retrieved sample number.
Figure 1(a) shows performance scaling with memory size, and Figure 1(b) shows scaling with retrieved samples. Table 3 evaluates on out-of-domain GUI environments.

predictive_evaluation.evidence_quote_or_locator: Section 2.2.1 and 2.2.2 describe graph-organized memory systems. AriGraph (Anokhin et al., 2024) is cited as integrating 'semantic and episodic memories within a unified graph framework' and is 'embedded within the Ariadne cognitive system.' The paper states these graph-based memory architectures enable 'efficient recall of relevant past experiences' and support 'multi-hop reasoning,' contrasting with unstructured approaches.
predictive_evaluation.evidence_in_support: The paper discusses multiple graph-organized memory systems (A-MEM, AriGraph) that structure agent experiences and knowledge as interconnected nodes and edges, enabling efficient retrieval and reasoning. The paper explicitly states that graph-based memory 'enables agents to efficiently recall relevant past experiences, identify patterns across interactions, and leverage historical context for better decision-making' compared to unstructured alternatives.

predictive_evaluation.evidence_quote_or_locator: Section 2.2 discusses memory retrieval mechanisms in graph-organized systems, mentioning that graphs 'enable efficient retrieval of contextually relevant information' and 'support multi-hop reasoning.' However, the paper does not provide direct empirical comparisons with quantitative metrics between selective retrieval and full-history baselines.

Section 2.2.2 mentions AriGraph integrates 'semantic and episodic memories within a unified graph framework,' but does not provide ablation studies comparing hybrid approaches against single-modality baselines with quantitative performance metrics on text game tasks.

law.statement_name: Memory Structure, Selectivity, and Retrieval are as Critical as Memory Size or Modality
law.theory_statement: The benefit of memory augmentation to LLM agents in text games depends not just on the presence or volume of stored experience, but crucially on how memory is structured (graphs, attributes, summaries), accessed (contextual semantic retrieval, attribute filtering, embedding similarity, graph traversal), and composed/interleaved with ongoing reasoning. Unstructured, brute-force context window or naive concatenation yields information overload or sparsity and fails to scale in complex games.
law.domain_scope: LLM-based agents with any form of persistent memory in settings with nontrivial state/action space, partial observability, and long or branching planning horizons.
special_cases: In trivial or small-scale settings where history fits in the prompt, advanced retrieval offers little marginal gain.
In environments with no discoverable structure or pure randomness, selectivity/structure add overhead but little task benefit.

supporting_evidence.text: Worldformer’s graph-diff representation and set-of-sequences (SOS) loss, RAP’s windowed trajectory retrieval, MemInsight’s attribute-based and embedding retrieval, and MC-DML’s distinction between context window and cross-trial memory all yield large performance improvements over naive retrieval or memory-dumping agents.
Ariadne/AriGraph's ablations (full-history, summaries, RAG, Simulacra) and unstructured context concatenation produce much lower performance and poor scaling to complex or long tasks.
ThinkThrice and Sweet&Sour explicitly leverage retrieval, selective reflection, and verification to outperform memoryless or unselective/naive retrieval agents.

GATA, KG-A2C, and MoE agents' use of graph-based masking, attention, and value-seeded mixing demonstrates the criticality of retrieval mechanisms and selective structuring, as opposed to raw memory augmentation.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Importance of retrieval is acknowledged in information retrieval, RAG, and some memory-augmented LM architectures; information overload in full context is a recognized issue.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: This general law formalizes the equivalence (or superiority) of memory structure and access relative to raw memory volume or memory type for performance/goals in complex reasoning environments, based on unified evidence.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Evidence exists in various ablations and isolated system papers, but this theory codifies it at a high level and ties it to both failure and success modes systematically.
references: Borji et al. (2023) A Survey on Large Language Model-Based Game Agents [retrieval cited, not law]
Meng et al. (2025) MemInsight [attribute/embedding retrieval]
Yao et al. (2023) RAG survey and retrieval theory

predictive_rubric.specific_prediction: LLM agents using structured memory representations (e.g., knowledge graphs, belief graphs, attribute-indexed retrieval) outperform agents using unstructured memory (e.g., naive concatenation of full history or raw episodic logs) on complex text games with non-trivial state spaces and partial observability.
operational_signals: Direct comparison between structured memory architectures (graphs, schemas, attributes) and unstructured/concatenation baselines
Performance metrics such as task completion rate, score, success rate, or goal achievement in text games
Ablation studies removing or replacing structured memory with full-history or unstructured approaches
predictive_rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must directly compare a structured memory method against an unstructured or naive concatenation baseline on the same text game environment, reporting quantitative task performance metrics.
predictive_rubric.what_does_support_look_like: Structured memory agents achieve significantly higher task completion rates, scores, or success rates than unstructured/naive concatenation baselines in complex or long-horizon text games.
predictive_rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Unstructured or naive concatenation methods match or exceed the performance of structured memory methods on complex text games with non-trivial state spaces.

predictive_rubric.specific_prediction: LLM agents with selective, contextual memory retrieval mechanisms (e.g., semantic similarity, attribute filtering, graph traversal) outperform agents that use brute-force context inclusion or no retrieval filtering in text games requiring planning and reasoning.
operational_signals: Comparison between selective/contextual retrieval methods and full-history or no-retrieval baselines
Explicit ablation of retrieval mechanisms (e.g., removing semantic retrieval, attribute filtering)
Performance metrics in text games, particularly those with long horizons or large state spaces
predictive_rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must compare an agent with selective retrieval (e.g., embedding-based, attribute-based, or graph-based selection) against a baseline using full context or no retrieval on the same task, with quantitative performance metrics.
predictive_rubric.what_does_support_look_like: Selective retrieval agents demonstrate higher task performance and better scaling to complex or long-horizon games compared to full-history or no-retrieval baselines.
predictive_rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Full-history inclusion or no-retrieval baselines achieve equal or superior performance to selective retrieval methods in complex text games.

predictive_rubric.specific_prediction: LLM agents that combine structured memory (e.g., graphs, schemas) with episodic/reflection-based memory outperform agents relying solely on one memory modality (structured-only or episodic-only) in complex text games.
operational_signals: Ablation studies comparing hybrid memory systems against structured-only or episodic-only variants
Explicit architectural comparison of combined vs. single-modality memory systems
Performance metrics in text games with partial observability and long planning horizons
predictive_rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must compare a hybrid memory architecture (combining structured and episodic/reflection components) against single-modality baselines (structured-only or episodic-only) on the same text game task with quantitative metrics.
predictive_rubric.what_does_support_look_like: Hybrid memory systems achieve higher task completion rates or scores than either structured-only or episodic-only memory systems in complex text games.
predictive_rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Single-modality memory systems (structured-only or episodic-only) match or exceed the performance of hybrid memory architectures in complex text games.

predictive_rubric.specific_prediction: As text game complexity increases (larger state/action spaces, longer horizons, more partial observability), the performance gap between structured/selective memory agents and unstructured/naive memory agents widens in favor of structured/selective approaches.
operational_signals: Performance comparisons across multiple difficulty levels or game complexities
Evaluation on simple vs. complex game variants with varying state space sizes or horizon lengths
Explicit analysis of scaling behavior as task complexity increases
predictive_rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must evaluate both structured/selective and unstructured/naive memory approaches across at least two distinct complexity levels of text games, showing performance trends as complexity increases.
predictive_rubric.what_does_support_look_like: The performance advantage of structured/selective memory over unstructured/naive methods increases as game complexity (state space, horizon, partial observability) increases.
predictive_rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Unstructured/naive memory methods maintain equal performance gaps or close the gap with structured/selective methods as game complexity increases.

predictive_rubric.specific_prediction: In text games where full history fits within the LLM context window and state spaces are small, advanced structured memory and selective retrieval provide minimal or no performance improvement over simple full-history inclusion.
operational_signals: Comparison of advanced memory architectures vs. full-history baselines on small-scale or simple text games
Explicit evaluation on tasks where context limits are not reached
Performance metrics showing marginal differences in low-complexity settings
predictive_rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must compare advanced memory/retrieval methods against full-history baselines on trivial or small-scale text games where history fits in context, reporting performance metrics that show minimal or no advantage.
predictive_rubric.what_does_support_look_like: In small-scale games where full history fits in context, advanced memory structures show minimal (<5% relative) or no performance improvement over full-history baselines.
predictive_rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Advanced memory structures show substantial (>10% relative) performance gains over full-history baselines even in trivial settings where all history fits in context.

predictive_evaluation.evidence_quote_or_locator: Table II shows BudgetMem (selective 30% budget storage) achieves F1=0.8042 with 72.4% memory savings vs. Baseline (100% storage) F1=0.8123 on long documents. Table III demonstrates this effect across document lengths.
predictive_evaluation.evidence_in_support: BudgetMem with selective salience-based storage demonstrates 72.4% memory savings with only 1.0% F1 degradation on long documents (5K-10K tokens) compared to baseline full-storage RAG, showing selective retrieval outperforms brute-force context inclusion under resource constraints.

predictive_evaluation.evidence_quote_or_locator: Table III: Short documents (<500 tok): 9.7% F1 drop, 15.5% memory savings. Long documents (>5K tok): 1.0% F1 drop, 72.4% memory savings. Figure 1 and Section V-C analyze this scaling effect.
predictive_evaluation.evidence_in_support: The performance advantage of selective memory over full storage increases dramatically with document length: memory savings increase from 15.5% (short) to 72.4% (long) while F1 degradation decreases from 9.7% to 1.0%, demonstrating that the gap widens in favor of selective approaches as complexity increases.

individual_paper_evaluations.response_text:
# Systematic Evaluation

I will evaluate each prediction by carefully examining whether the paper directly tests it with quantitative comparisons.

## Prediction 1: structured_vs_unstructured_memory

**Analysis:** The paper compares BudgetMem (which uses feature-based salience scoring and selective storage) against a "Baseline RAG" that stores all chunks. However, this is NOT a test of structured vs. unstructured memory representations. Both systems use the same chunking approach and BM25 retrieval. The difference is in *selection* (what to store), not in *structure* (graphs vs. concatenation). The paper does not compare knowledge graphs, belief graphs, or attribute-indexed retrieval against naive concatenation baselines.

**Strength:** weak - The paper addresses memory efficiency but not structured vs. unstructured representations.

## Prediction 2: selective_vs_brute_force_retrieval

**Analysis:** The paper directly compares selective memory storage (BudgetMem with 30% budget using salience-based selection) against full storage baseline. Tables I and II show quantitative performance metrics (F1 scores) comparing these approaches. On long documents: Baseline stores 100% of chunks, BudgetMem stores 27.6% (72.4% savings), with only 1.0% F1 drop (Table II). This is a direct test of selective vs. brute-force storage with quantitative metrics.

**Strength:** strong - Direct comparison with quantitative metrics on the same tasks.

## Prediction 3: hybrid_vs_single_modality_memory

**Analysis:** The paper describes a "dual-tier memory architecture" (episodic + semantic) in Section III-F, but this is part of the "Full Architectural Proposal" that was NOT implemented. Section III-C explicitly states: "Current Implementation (Evaluated in Section V): ... Single-tier memory without episodic/semantic separation." The experiments evaluate only feature-based selection without comparing hybrid vs. single-modality memory systems.

**Strength:** none - The dual-tier system was not implemented or tested.

## Prediction 4: memory_structure_scaling

**Analysis:** The paper explicitly evaluates performance across different document lengths. Table III shows results for short (<500 tokens, N=500) vs. long (>5K tokens, N=200) documents. For short documents: 9.7% F1 drop with 15.5% memory savings. For long documents: 1.0% F1 drop with 72.4% memory savings. This demonstrates that the performance gap (measured as memory savings while maintaining F1) widens as complexity increases, favoring the selective approach.

**Strength:** strong - Direct comparison across complexity levels with quantitative metrics.

## Prediction 5: trivial_setting_boundary

**Analysis:** The paper evaluates on short documents (SQuAD, avg 237 tokens) where most content could fit in context windows. Table I shows that BudgetMem achieves only 15.5% memory savings with a 9.7% F1 drop on these short documents. However, this is NOT testing "advanced structured memory and selective retrieval" providing minimal improvement - rather, it shows substantial degradation (9.7% F1 loss) for modest savings (15.5%). The paper does not compare against full-history baselines that include all content within context.

**Strength:** weak - Tests short documents but shows degradation rather than minimal advantage, and doesn't compare against full-history inclusion within context window.

law.theory_name: Hybrid Structured-Episodic Memory as a Fundamental Requirement for Robust Performance in LLM-Based Text Game Agents
law.theory_description: This theory posits that robust performance, generalization, and strategic flexibility in language model (LLM) agents across complex, partially observable text games require not just large memory but a principled integration of structured (e.g., knowledge/belief graph) and episodic/reflection-based memory. The synergy emerges because structured memory offers compact relational representations for navigation and reasoning, while episodic/reflection memory provides dynamic adaptation, procedural recall, and learning from both failure and success. Neither naive retention of all history nor pure reliance on unstructured episodic memory alone is sufficient for scaling or generalizing to new, difficult, or long-horizon tasks; selective retrieval and composition of these modalities is necessary to balance information compression, retrieval relevance, and depth of reasoning.

predictive_evaluation.evidence_quote_or_locator: Table 1 compares H-MEM (hierarchical structured memory with position indexing) against baselines including LoCoMo (LCM.) which uses 'complete previous historical interaction information combined with the question' without special memory mechanisms. Section 4.2 states H-MEM 'consistently achieves the highest average F1 and BLEU-1 scores across all model and task configurations, with improvements of 14.98 and 12.77 points over the baselines.'
predictive_evaluation.evidence_in_support: The paper directly compares H-MEM's hierarchical structured memory architecture (organizing memory into Domain, Category, Memory Trace, and Episode layers with position index encoding) against LoCoMo baseline which uses naive concatenation of full history. Table 1 shows H-MEM substantially outperforms the unstructured baseline across all tasks (e.g., on Qwen-1.5B: H-MEM achieves 30.37 F1 vs. LoCoMo's 14.95 F1 average). The ablation study (Figure 5) shows removing hierarchical structure (w/o H&R) causes significant performance degradation.

predictive_evaluation.evidence_quote_or_locator: Section 3.2 describes the selective retrieval mechanism: 'an index-based routing mechanism enables efficient, layer-by-layer retrieval without performing exhaustive similarity computations.' Table 2 and Figure 4 compare H-MEM's selective retrieval against MemoryBank's full similarity-based search, showing H-MEM is '5 times' faster. Ablation study (Figure 5) shows removing retrieval component (w/o R.) significantly degrades performance.
predictive_evaluation.evidence_in_support: The paper directly compares H-MEM's selective position index-based retrieval (which retrieves top-k at each hierarchical level using position indices to guide layer-by-layer search) against baseline approaches. Table 2 shows H-MEM achieves better F1/BLEU-1 scores than MemoryBank while using dramatically fewer compute operations (e.g., Adversarial task: H-MEM 4.38×10^7 ops vs. MemoryBank 7.34×10^9 ops). The ablation study demonstrates that removing the retrieval mechanism causes performance drops across all task types.

predictive_evaluation.evidence_quote_or_locator: Table 2 presents computational efficiency experiments where 'five task types—Single Hop, Multi Hop, Temporal, Open Domain, and Adversarial—are executed sequentially' with 'stored memory not cleared between task switches but continuously accumulated.' Figure 4 shows 'the calculation amount of baseline shows an almost exponential growth trend with the accumulation of memory, while H-MEM still shows a slow increase and gradually stabilizes trend.'
predictive_evaluation.evidence_in_support: The paper evaluates performance as memory complexity increases through sequential execution of tasks with accumulating memory. Table 2 shows the performance gap widens dramatically: for simpler Single Hop tasks, H-MEM outperforms MemoryBank by ~9 F1 points with 2.6x fewer operations; but for complex Adversarial tasks with maximum accumulated memory, H-MEM outperforms by ~23 F1 points with 168x fewer operations. This demonstrates the advantage of structured/selective memory increases as complexity and memory size grows.

Neither naive retention of all history nor pure reliance on unstructured episodic memory alone is sufficient for scaling or generalizing to new, difficult, or long-horizon tasks; selective retrieval and composition of these modalities is necessary to balance information compression, retrieval relevance, and depth of reasoning.

law.statement_name: Memory Structure, Selectivity, and Retrieval are as Critical as Memory Size or Modality
law.theory_statement: The benefit of memory augmentation to LLM agents in text games depends not just on the presence or volume of stored experience, but crucially on how memory is structured (graphs, attributes, summaries), accessed (contextual semantic retrieval, attribute filtering, embedding similarity, graph traversal), and composed/interleaved with ongoing reasoning. Unstructured, brute-force context window or naive concatenation yields information overload or sparsity and fails to scale in complex games.
law.domain_scope: LLM-based agents with any form of persistent memory in settings with nontrivial state/action space, partial observability, and long or branching planning horizons.
special_cases: In trivial or small-scale settings where history fits in the prompt, advanced retrieval offers little marginal gain.
In environments with no discoverable structure or pure randomness, selectivity/structure add overhead but little task benefit.

supporting_evidence.text: Worldformer’s graph-diff representation and set-of-sequences (SOS) loss, RAP’s windowed trajectory retrieval, MemInsight’s attribute-based and embedding retrieval, and MC-DML’s distinction between context window and cross-trial memory all yield large performance improvements over naive retrieval or memory-dumping agents.
Ariadne/AriGraph's ablations (full-history, summaries, RAG, Simulacra) and unstructured context concatenation produce much lower performance and poor scaling to complex or long tasks.
ThinkThrice and Sweet&Sour explicitly leverage retrieval, selective reflection, and verification to outperform memoryless or unselective/naive retrieval agents.

GATA, KG-A2C, and MoE agents' use of graph-based masking, attention, and value-seeded mixing demonstrates the criticality of retrieval mechanisms and selective structuring, as opposed to raw memory augmentation.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Importance of retrieval is acknowledged in information retrieval, RAG, and some memory-augmented LM architectures; information overload in full context is a recognized issue.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: This general law formalizes the equivalence (or superiority) of memory structure and access relative to raw memory volume or memory type for performance/goals in complex reasoning environments, based on unified evidence.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Evidence exists in various ablations and isolated system papers, but this theory codifies it at a high level and ties it to both failure and success modes systematically.
references: Borji et al. (2023) A Survey on Large Language Model-Based Game Agents [retrieval cited, not law]
Meng et al. (2025) MemInsight [attribute/embedding retrieval]
Yao et al. (2023) RAG survey and retrieval theory

predictive_rubric.specific_prediction: LLM agents using structured memory representations (e.g., knowledge graphs, belief graphs, attribute-indexed retrieval) outperform agents using unstructured memory (e.g., naive concatenation of full history or raw episodic logs) on complex text games with non-trivial state spaces and partial observability.
operational_signals: Direct comparison between structured memory architectures (graphs, schemas, attributes) and unstructured/concatenation baselines
Performance metrics such as task completion rate, score, success rate, or goal achievement in text games
Ablation studies removing or replacing structured memory with full-history or unstructured approaches
predictive_rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must directly compare a structured memory method against an unstructured or naive concatenation baseline on the same text game environment, reporting quantitative task performance metrics.
predictive_rubric.what_does_support_look_like: Structured memory agents achieve significantly higher task completion rates, scores, or success rates than unstructured/naive concatenation baselines in complex or long-horizon text games.
predictive_rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Unstructured or naive concatenation methods match or exceed the performance of structured memory methods on complex text games with non-trivial state spaces.

predictive_rubric.specific_prediction: LLM agents with selective, contextual memory retrieval mechanisms (e.g., semantic similarity, attribute filtering, graph traversal) outperform agents that use brute-force context inclusion or no retrieval filtering in text games requiring planning and reasoning.
operational_signals: Comparison between selective/contextual retrieval methods and full-history or no-retrieval baselines
Explicit ablation of retrieval mechanisms (e.g., removing semantic retrieval, attribute filtering)
Performance metrics in text games, particularly those with long horizons or large state spaces
predictive_rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must compare an agent with selective retrieval (e.g., embedding-based, attribute-based, or graph-based selection) against a baseline using full context or no retrieval on the same task, with quantitative performance metrics.
predictive_rubric.what_does_support_look_like: Selective retrieval agents demonstrate higher task performance and better scaling to complex or long-horizon games compared to full-history or no-retrieval baselines.
predictive_rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Full-history inclusion or no-retrieval baselines achieve equal or superior performance to selective retrieval methods in complex text games.

predictive_rubric.specific_prediction: LLM agents that combine structured memory (e.g., graphs, schemas) with episodic/reflection-based memory outperform agents relying solely on one memory modality (structured-only or episodic-only) in complex text games.
operational_signals: Ablation studies comparing hybrid memory systems against structured-only or episodic-only variants
Explicit architectural comparison of combined vs. single-modality memory systems
Performance metrics in text games with partial observability and long planning horizons
predictive_rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must compare a hybrid memory architecture (combining structured and episodic/reflection components) against single-modality baselines (structured-only or episodic-only) on the same text game task with quantitative metrics.
predictive_rubric.what_does_support_look_like: Hybrid memory systems achieve higher task completion rates or scores than either structured-only or episodic-only memory systems in complex text games.
predictive_rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Single-modality memory systems (structured-only or episodic-only) match or exceed the performance of hybrid memory architectures in complex text games.

predictive_rubric.specific_prediction: As text game complexity increases (larger state/action spaces, longer horizons, more partial observability), the performance gap between structured/selective memory agents and unstructured/naive memory agents widens in favor of structured/selective approaches.
operational_signals: Performance comparisons across multiple difficulty levels or game complexities
Evaluation on simple vs. complex game variants with varying state space sizes or horizon lengths
Explicit analysis of scaling behavior as task complexity increases
predictive_rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must evaluate both structured/selective and unstructured/naive memory approaches across at least two distinct complexity levels of text games, showing performance trends as complexity increases.
predictive_rubric.what_does_support_look_like: The performance advantage of structured/selective memory over unstructured/naive methods increases as game complexity (state space, horizon, partial observability) increases.
predictive_rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Unstructured/naive memory methods maintain equal performance gaps or close the gap with structured/selective methods as game complexity increases.

predictive_rubric.specific_prediction: In text games where full history fits within the LLM context window and state spaces are small, advanced structured memory and selective retrieval provide minimal or no performance improvement over simple full-history inclusion.
operational_signals: Comparison of advanced memory architectures vs. full-history baselines on small-scale or simple text games
Explicit evaluation on tasks where context limits are not reached
Performance metrics showing marginal differences in low-complexity settings
predictive_rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must compare advanced memory/retrieval methods against full-history baselines on trivial or small-scale text games where history fits in context, reporting performance metrics that show minimal or no advantage.
predictive_rubric.what_does_support_look_like: In small-scale games where full history fits in context, advanced memory structures show minimal (<5% relative) or no performance improvement over full-history baselines.
predictive_rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Advanced memory structures show substantial (>10% relative) performance gains over full-history baselines even in trivial settings where all history fits in context.

predictive_evaluation.evidence_quote_or_locator: Table 2, Section 4.2, and Section 4.3.1. The paper compares 'Long-Context Agents' (treating full input as memory/unstructured) against 'Structure-Augmented RAG Agents' (using graphs, hierarchies) across multiple text-based tasks.
predictive_evaluation.evidence_in_support: Table 2 shows that Structure-Augmented RAG agents like HippoRAG-v2 achieve 65.1% average on AR tasks, outperforming the unstructured long-context baseline GPT-4o-mini (49.2%). The paper states 'Most RAG Agents are better than the backbone model GPT-4o-mini in the tasks within the Accurate Retrieval Category' and demonstrates that structured methods like HippoRAG-v2 achieve competitive or superior performance compared to naive context concatenation approaches.

predictive_evaluation.evidence_quote_or_locator: Table 2, Section 4.2, Table 8 (Appendix D.3), and Section 4.3.2. The paper compares RAG agents with selective retrieval (BM25, embedding-based, graph-based) against Long-Context agents using full-history inclusion.
predictive_evaluation.evidence_in_support: Table 2 demonstrates that selective retrieval agents (e.g., BM25 at 60.5% AR average, HippoRAG-v2 at 65.1%) outperform or match the performance of full-context baselines (GPT-4o-mini at 49.2% AR average) on accurate retrieval tasks. Section 4.3.2 shows that 'increasing the number of retrieved chunks generally improves performance across most tasks,' supporting the value of selective retrieval over brute-force inclusion.

predictive_evaluation.evidence_quote_or_locator: Table 9 (Appendix D.4) shows performance across different context lengths, and Figure 2 shows chunk size variations, but these do not constitute direct tests of the scaling prediction.
Table 9 shows performance at different context lengths including shorter contexts, but does not explicitly test the boundary condition where advanced methods show minimal advantage.

law.theory_name: Hybrid Structured-Episodic Memory as a Fundamental Requirement for Robust Performance in LLM-Based Text Game Agents
law.theory_description: This theory posits that robust performance, generalization, and strategic flexibility in language model (LLM) agents across complex, partially observable text games require not just large memory but a principled integration of structured (e.g., knowledge/belief graph) and episodic/reflection-based memory. The synergy emerges because structured memory offers compact relational representations for navigation and reasoning, while episodic/reflection memory provides dynamic adaptation, procedural recall, and learning from both failure and success. Neither naive retention of all history nor pure reliance on unstructured episodic memory alone is sufficient for scaling or generalizing to new, difficult, or long-horizon tasks; selective retrieval and composition of these modalities is necessary to balance information compression, retrieval relevance, and depth of reasoning.

predictive_evaluation.evidence_quote_or_locator: Table 1 shows performance comparison between COSMIR (structured memory with Extract/Infer/Refine phases), CoA (free-form message passing), and TC (truncated context). Section 2 and Appendix A.1 provide explicit failure analysis of unstructured CoA summaries losing information.
predictive_evaluation.evidence_in_support: COSMIR, which uses structured memory (Questions, Gathered Facts, Inferred Facts components per Equation 1), consistently outperforms CoA baseline across all three datasets and all three models in Table 1. For example, on InfBench-QA with GPT-4.1, COSMIR achieves 50.74 ROUGE-F1 vs CoA's 47.62. Section 2 explicitly demonstrates how COSMIR's structured memory preserves cross-chunk references while CoA's unstructured summaries lose critical information.

predictive_evaluation.evidence_quote_or_locator: Table 1 compares COSMIR (selective extraction with memory budget k) against TC baseline (truncated full-history). Section 4.1 specifies 'the size of F_g is constrained to at most a k-fraction of the length of a chunk', and Section 4.2 describes Extract phase selecting 'evidence units relevant to the user query q and current question set Q'.
predictive_evaluation.evidence_in_support: COSMIR with selective extraction (guided by question set Q and memory budget k) substantially outperforms the TC baseline which uses truncated full-history inclusion. For GPT-4.1 on InfBench-QA, COSMIR achieves 50.74 vs TC's 36.05 ROUGE-F1. The Extract phase explicitly performs selective retrieval by extracting only relevant evidence under budget constraints, with oldest facts pruned when budget is exceeded (Section 4.2).

predictive_evaluation.evidence_quote_or_locator: Table 1 shows results across three different datasets (InfBench-QA, InfBench-MC, NarrativeQA-256k) which have varying complexity. Section 6 notes 'The largest gains of COSMIR over CoA are seen for InfBench-QA and NarrativeQA-256k, which are free-form question-response benchmarks.'

predictive_evaluation.evidence_quote_or_locator: Table 5(c) DeepResearcher shows Memento (with structured case-based memory) achieves F1=66.6/PM=80.4 vs. Online Executor (unstructured, no memory) F1=30.8/PM=60.7. Section 4.2 describes structured case memory with Write/Read operations and parametric Q-function.
predictive_evaluation.evidence_in_support: The paper directly compares Memento's structured case memory system (storing task-action-reward triplets with Q-function-based retrieval) against an Online Executor baseline that lacks structured memory. Across DeepResearcher benchmarks, Memento achieves substantial gains (F1: +35.8 points, PM: +19.7 points), demonstrating that structured memory representations outperform unstructured approaches in complex text-based research tasks.

predictive_evaluation.evidence_quote_or_locator: Table 3 shows performance across different K values (retrieval count). K=4 achieves highest F1=64.5 and PM=78.5, while K=0 (no retrieval) scores F1=59.9/PM=72.2. Section 4.2 describes selective retrieval using Q-function and semantic similarity.
predictive_evaluation.evidence_in_support: The paper explicitly evaluates selective retrieval mechanisms (Q-function-based case selection and semantic similarity) against no-retrieval baselines. Table 3 demonstrates that selective retrieval with K=4 cases yields optimal performance (F1=64.5, PM=78.5) compared to K=0 (no retrieval: F1=59.9, PM=72.2), showing that selective retrieval outperforms brute-force context inclusion in complex research tasks.

predictive_evaluation.evidence_quote_or_locator: The paper describes a case-based memory system with both parametric (Q-function) and non-parametric (similarity-based) components, but does not explicitly compare hybrid vs. single-modality episodic or structured memory as defined in the prediction.

Table 2 shows GAIA performance across three difficulty levels. Memento achieves Level 1: 96.23%, Level 2: 90.70%, Level 3: 61.54%. Figure 1d shows OOD generalization gains of 4.7% to 9.6%. Table 5(c) compares Memento vs. baselines across complexity.
predictive_evaluation.evidence_in_support: The paper evaluates Memento across GAIA's three difficulty levels (Level 1: simple, Level 2: moderate, Level 3: complex with up to 50 steps). While absolute performance decreases with complexity (as expected), the relative advantage of Memento's structured memory over baselines remains consistent or increases. Table 5(c) shows larger performance gaps for Memento vs. Online Executor on complex multi-hop datasets (e.g., Musique: +17.6 F1, +13.6 PM), supporting that structured memory's advantage scales with task complexity.
